feynman.0: You’re going to design your own investigation — build it, run it, and find something out for yourself. It generates primary data on fields, motion or light, using one continuous independent variable, and at the end you write it all up as a scientific poster. So here’s what this chapter walks through: how to design the thing, how to judge whether your measurements are any good, how to process and graph what you collect, and how to land a conclusion you can actually defend — all in the conventions you’re expected to use.
feynman.1: Every investigation starts with one clear thing: an investigation question — a specific, answerable question about how two measurable quantities relate to each other. That question becomes the title of your poster. Once you have it, the rest follows: the aim, which is just a plain statement of what you’re setting out to find, and the relevant physics concepts and key definitions, pulled from the study design so that what you build rests on theory that’s already solid.
feynman.2: A hypothesis is your testable, falsifiable guess at how the variables relate — written down before you collect any data, and backed up by the physics you already know. It has to commit to a direction or a shape: say, that the period of a pendulum gets longer as the pendulum gets longer, or that the magnetic field strength drops off as you move away from a magnet. A prediction is a bit different — it says what you expect this particular procedure to spit out.
feynman.3: An investigation here is all about testing how one quantity rides on another, so before you measure a single thing, you sort out three kinds of variable.
feynman.4: Key idea You change the independent variable, you measure the dependent variable, and you hold every controlled variable dead still. With one continuous independent variable, you measure the dependent variable at a whole series of values across a range — that’s what lets you graph the relationship.
feynman.5: The methodology is the overall kind of scientific investigation you pick; the method is the actual step-by-step procedure you go through. When you’ve got one continuous independent variable, the methodology that fits is a controlled experiment: you vary the independent variable across a range while keeping every controlled variable nailed down, and you measure the dependent variable at each value. The method spells out the gear, which values of the independent variable you’ll test, how many repeated measurements you take at each one, and which instruments you use.
feynman.6: What the method gives you is primary data — data you collected with your own hands, first-hand. It comes in two flavours. It can be quantitative (numbers you measured, like a time in seconds or a field strength in millitesla), or it can be qualitative (things you noticed that aren’t numbers, like the colour of a fringe or whether a surface is reflective). An investigation with a continuous independent variable leans on quantitative primary data, with the qualitative stuff jotted down wherever it helps the analysis.
feynman.7: Before you collect anything, you bring in the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines. A risk assessment names the hazards and the controls that tame them: eye protection where there are projectiles or lasers, safe handling of masses and electrical supplies, and limits on laser power and exposure. And ethics asks something simple but non-negotiable — you record every result honestly, including the ones that don’t match your hypothesis, and you give credit for every source and every bit of help you got.
feynman.8: All of it — every measurement, observation, procedure, and every change you make to the method — goes into a logbook the moment it happens. You date it and keep it in order. That’s what proves the primary data is genuinely your own first-hand work, and it’s the record you come back to later when you process and report your results.
feynman.9: Connects The physics you’re investigating comes straight out of Units 3 and 4: a fields investigation might measure field strength against distance (topic 5, topic 7), a motion investigation might measure acceleration or period, and a light investigation might measure fringe spacing or intensity (topic 13). Whichever one you pick, the design conventions on this page still apply.
feynman.10: Every measurement gets sized up against the true value: the value you’d find if you could somehow measure the quantity perfectly. You can never know it exactly — that’s the catch — but the language of measurement quality lets you talk about how close your result is likely to be to it, and how you got there.
feynman.11: Accuracy is a word-picture, not a number: a measurement is accurate if it sits close to the true value. You say more accurate or less accurate; you never put a number on it. Precision is something else entirely — it’s how tightly your repeated measurements bunch together, the spread of the readings. Precision tells you nothing about whether you’re near the true value, so it’s a separate thing from accuracy. You can have a set of readings that’s precise but inaccurate: they agree beautifully with each other and yet they’re all sitting way off the true value.
feynman.12: Trap Accuracy and precision are not the same word wearing two hats — don’t swap them. Precision is how well your repeated measurements agree with each other; accuracy is how close you are to the true value. A tight little cluster of readings is precise; whether it’s also accurate depends entirely on where the true value happens to be. Say accuracy in words (more or less accurate) and never as a number.
feynman.13: The resolution of an instrument is the smallest change in the thing you’re measuring that actually shows up as a change in the reading. A millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm; a stopwatch reading to 0.01 s has a resolution of 0.01 s. Resolution is just a property of the scale — and it’s not the same thing as the uncertainty of a measurement you make with that instrument.
feynman.14: A measurement error is the gap between what you measured and the true value. Uncertainty is a different beast: it’s the interval you expect the true value to fall inside. When you write a result as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty — say 20 ± 1 mm — you’re saying you expect the true value to lie somewhere between 19 mm and 21 mm. On a graph, you draw that interval as an uncertainty bar.
feynman.15: Trap Resolution is not uncertainty. A millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm, but by VCE convention its measurement uncertainty is half the smallest scale increment, ±0.5 mm. Calling the uncertainty the whole increment, or muddling the two words together, is a mistake people make all the time.
feynman.16: There are two kinds of error, and they mess with your measurements in completely different ways.
feynman.17: Trap Averaging only cuts down random error. Systematic error shoves every reading the same way, so piling up more readings and averaging them does nothing to it. The only way to get rid of a systematic error is to track down its cause and fix it — zero the instrument, say, or get rid of parallax.
feynman.18: Repeatability is how closely successive measurements agree when you keep everything the same: same observer, same instrument, same method, same place, all in a short stretch of time. Repeatability is about precision. Reproducibility is how closely things agree when you deliberately change the conditions: a different observer, instrument, method or location. Reproducibility puts your claimed precision to the test and can flush out systematic error — so it ties back to accuracy.
feynman.19: An investigation is valid if it actually investigates the thing it set out to investigate. That validity is in danger if you let the controlled variables drift, because then you can no longer pin the change in the dependent variable on the independent variable — or if observer bias creeps in, where what the observer expects to see quietly nudges the readings.
feynman.20: Key idea Random error scatters your readings and wrecks precision; you beat it down by repeating and averaging. Systematic error pushes every reading the same way and wrecks accuracy; averaging won’t touch it. Repeatability and reproducibility are about precision; validity is whether the experiment tests what it claims to test.
feynman.21: You state your calculated results to a number of significant figures that matches how precise your data really is. Two rules run the show.
feynman.22: You round only at the very last step. Along the way, you carry extra figures in the intermediate results so the rounding doesn’t pile up on you.
feynman.23: Trap The two significant-figure rules aren’t interchangeable. Addition and subtraction go by decimal places; multiplication and division go by significant figures. And rounding at every intermediate step instead of only at the end bakes in error you didn’t have to have.
feynman.24: By VCE convention, the uncertainty of a reading you take with an analogue or digital instrument is half its smallest scale increment. A millimetre ruler has a smallest increment of 1 mm, so the uncertainty is ±0.5 mm. A stopwatch reading to 0.01 s has an uncertainty of ±0.005 s. And you report the result as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty:
feynman.25: The percentage uncertainty writes the uncertainty as a fraction of the measurement itself, which is handy — it lets you compare the relative quality of measurements that aren’t even the same size:
feynman.26: Say you measure a length with a millimetre ruler and get 152 mm. The smallest scale increment is 1 mm, so the uncertainty is half of that, ±0.5 mm.
feynman.27: Result: the length is 152.0 ± 0.5 mm. You expect the true value to lie somewhere between 151.5 mm and 152.5 mm.
feynman.28: Percentage uncertainty: 0.5152.0 × 100 = 0.33%, stated to two significant figures.
feynman.29: A trolley runs 1.27 m in 0.42 s, and you want the average speed. It’s a division, so the answer keeps the fewest significant figures of the numbers you put in. The 1.27 m has three significant figures and the 0.42 s has two, so the answer comes out to two significant figures.
feynman.30: Speed = 1.270.42 = 3.0238… = 3.0 m s⁻¹. You round only at this final step, not before.
feynman.31: Key idea Write every result as a mean ± uncertainty, with the uncertainty equal to half the smallest scale increment. Use decimal places when you add or subtract and significant figures when you multiply or divide — and round only at the very end.
feynman.32: A graph hands you the relationship between the independent and dependent variables. The independent variable goes on the horizontal axis, the dependent variable on the vertical axis. You label each axis with its quantity and unit, and scale it so the data spread out and fill the space. Every plotted point carries uncertainty bars: a vertical bar for the uncertainty in the dependent variable and a horizontal bar for the uncertainty in the independent variable. You expect the true value of each point to sit somewhere inside its bars.
feynman.33: When the relationship is linear, you draw a line of best fit — one that comes as close as it can to all the points, with the points scattered evenly above and below it. It should pass within the uncertainty bars of the points. And there are a few ways to get it wrong, so watch out: don’t draw the line through just the first and last points, don’t force it through some chosen point or through the origin, and don’t run it out past the range of your data.
feynman.34: Trap A line of best fit isn’t drawn through the first and last points only, isn’t forced through the origin, and isn’t extrapolated beyond the data. And here’s a nice bonus: if a line that theory says should go through the origin doesn’t, that offset is itself evidence of a systematic error.
feynman.35: When the raw variables don’t give you a straight line, you linearise: you transform one variable so that plotting the transformed data does give a straight line. You have to transform both the variable and its axis unit. If a quantity is expected to go as the square of the time, you square the time values and label the axis with the squared quantity and its unit — for example “time² (s²)”. Then the gradient of that linearised graph equals an actual physical quantity.
feynman.36: The gradient of a straight-line graph is the rise divided by the run, read off from two points that lie on the line and are picked far apart — not from points pulled out of the data table:
feynman.37: The units of the gradient are the units of the vertical axis divided by the units of the horizontal axis. If there’s a ×10ⁿ scale factor printed on an axis, you have to include it when you read the values off. And you don’t nudge the gradient to match some constant you were hoping for.
feynman.38: Trap Read the gradient from two widely spaced points that lie on the line of best fit, never from points in the data table that might not lie on the line at all. Include any ×10ⁿ axis scale factors, give the gradient its correct units (vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit), and don’t fiddle with the gradient to force it to agree with the value you expected.
feynman.39: The period T of a simple pendulum hangs on its length L through T = 2π√(L/g), so a plot of T against L comes out as a curve. But square it and you get T² = 4π²gL, which is linear in L. So you square the values of T and plot them up the vertical axis, labelled “period² (s²)”, against L in metres along the horizontal axis.
feynman.40: Pick two points on the line of best fit, far apart, and read them off as (0.20 m, 0.80 s²) and (1.00 m, 4.0 s²). The gradient is 4.0 − 0.801.00 − 0.20 = 3.20.80 = 4.0 s² m⁻¹, with units of vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit.
feynman.41: That gradient equals 4π²g, so g = 4π²gradient = 39.54.0 = 9.9 m s⁻², stated to two significant figures. And there it is — the gradient of a linearised graph has handed you a physical quantity.
feynman.42: Key idea Linearise by transforming the variable and its axis unit together, so the gradient of the straight line equals a physical quantity. Read the gradient as rise over run from two widely spaced points on the line of best fit, give it the correct units, and include any axis scale factors.
feynman.43: Your primary data is the evidence that bears on the hypothesis. You organise it, analyse it and evaluate it to find the patterns hiding in it; the gradient of linearised data, the trend of the line of best fit, and how the points scatter relative to their uncertainty bars — you read all of these as evidence. A conclusion says how strongly that evidence supports the hypothesis, but only after you’ve thought through the causes of uncertainty, the assumptions you made, and the limitations of your data and method.
feynman.44: Evidence can support a hypothesis, model or theory, or it can refute one — but it can never nail one down with certainty. So a conclusion says the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis. You don’t say “proves” or “disproves”, and here’s why: a finite pile of measurements simply can’t prove a general claim.
feynman.45: Trap A conclusion never says the evidence “proves” or “disproves” the hypothesis. The right words are that the evidence supports, partially supports, or does not support it. Evidence that partially supports a hypothesis is evidence that matches the predicted trend over part of the range, or matches the trend but not the predicted value.
feynman.46: The evaluation digs out the limitations of your investigation, the assumptions you leaned on, and the causes of uncertainty in your data — and then it proposes improvements that would deal with them. A limitation is a specific weakness, like a too-narrow range of the independent variable or a controlled variable you couldn’t quite hold still; an improvement is a concrete change that would cut its effect down.
feynman.47: You report the investigation using the conventions of science communication — and these matter: the right terminology, symbols, equations and standard abbreviations; units kept consistent; and results stated to a sensible number of significant figures. You write quantities with their units in upright form, like m s⁻¹, and uncertainties with the ± symbol.
feynman.48: The whole thing is assessed as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. Right at its heart sits a single Communication statement: one sentence that answers the investigation question. That statement takes up 20–25% of the poster area. And the poster carries the prescribed sections.
feynman.49: On a poster you have to be lean — the word limit means only the essential stuff makes the cut, and every reference and every source of help gets acknowledged. So you explain the key findings and what they mean clearly enough that a reader can judge for themselves how the evidence answers the question.
feynman.50: Key idea The poster answers the investigation question in a single central Communication statement, backed by results that show the trend and a discussion that weighs the evidence. The conclusion says the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis — all within a limit of 600 words and the prescribed sections.

sagan.0: A student-designed practical investigation is a small act of original science: with your own hands you generate primary data on fields, motion or light, steering a single continuous independent variable, and the whole of that effort is gathered into a scientific poster. This chapter is your guide to the journey — how to design the investigation, how to weigh the quality of every measurement you make, how to process and graph what you find, and how to speak a defensible conclusion in the conventions that science has agreed upon.
sagan.1: Every scientific investigation begins, as all real questions do, with a clearly stated investigation question: a specific, answerable question about the relationship between two measurable quantities. That question becomes the title of your poster — the banner under which all your work will stand. From it follow the aim, a plain statement of what the investigation sets out to determine, and the relevant physics concepts and key definitions, drawn from the study design so that your design rests on the firm ground of established theory.
sagan.2: A hypothesis is a testable, falsifiable prediction of the relationship between the variables — a guess made honest, spoken before any data are collected and justified by the background physics. A hypothesis must commit itself to a direction or a form: that the period of a pendulum increases with its length, say, or that the magnetic field strength decreases with distance from a magnet. A prediction goes further still, stating the expected outcome of the one specific procedure you intend to follow.
sagan.3: An investigation in this area of study asks how one quantity depends upon another, and so, before a single measurement is taken, three classes of variable must be named.
sagan.4: Key idea The investigator changes the independent variable, measures the dependent variable, and holds every controlled variable steadfastly constant. With one continuous independent variable, the dependent variable is measured at a whole series of values across a range, so that a relationship — a pattern hidden in the numbers — can at last be graphed and seen.
sagan.5: The methodology is the overall type of scientific investigation you choose; the method is the specific step-by-step procedure you actually carry out. For an investigation with one continuous independent variable, the fitting methodology is a controlled experiment: the independent variable is varied across a range while all controlled variables are held constant, and the dependent variable is measured at each value. The method then sets down the particulars — the equipment, the values of the independent variable to be tested, the number of repeated measurements at each value, and the instruments used.
sagan.6: The method yields primary data, data gathered first-hand by the investigator — by you, and no one else. Primary data may be quantitative (numerical measurements, such as a time in seconds or a field strength in millitesla) or qualitative (observed properties that are not numerical, such as the colour of a fringe or whether a surface is reflective). An investigation with a continuous independent variable leans upon quantitative primary data, with qualitative observations recorded wherever they lend support to the analysis.
sagan.7: Before any data are collected, the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines are brought to bear. A risk assessment names the hazards and the controls that reduce them: eye protection where projectiles or lasers are in play, safe handling of masses and electrical supplies, and limits on laser power and exposure. Ethical conduct demands the honest recording of every result — including, and especially, those that defy the hypothesis — and the acknowledgement of all sources and assistance. This honesty is the very bedrock on which science stands.
sagan.8: All primary data, observations, procedures and changes to the method are written into a logbook at the very moment they occur. The logbook is dated and kept in chronological order; it authenticates the primary data as the investigator’s own first-hand work, and it stands as the record from which results are later processed and reported — a faithful diary of the search.
sagan.9: Connects The physics you investigate is drawn from Units 3 and 4: a fields investigation may measure field strength against distance (topic 5, topic 7), a motion investigation may measure acceleration or period, and a light investigation may measure fringe spacing or intensity (topic 13). The design conventions on this page hold true for whichever context you choose to explore.
sagan.10: Every measurement is held up against the true value: the value that would be found if the quantity could be measured perfectly, without flaw. The true value can never be known exactly — it stands forever just beyond our reach — yet the language of measurement quality lets us describe how close a result is likely to be to it, and by what means the result was won.
sagan.11: Accuracy is a qualitative description: a measurement is accurate if it falls close to the true value. Accuracy is spoken of as more accurate or less accurate; it is never stated as a number. Precision is something quite different — how close repeated measurements lie to one another, the spread of the readings. Precision tells us nothing of closeness to the true value, and so it stands apart from accuracy. A set of readings may be precise yet inaccurate, agreeing beautifully among themselves while all of them sit far from the truth.
sagan.12: Trap Accuracy and precision are not synonyms, and they must never be traded one for the other. Precision is the agreement among repeated measurements; accuracy is closeness to the true value. A tightly clustered set of readings is precise; whether it is also accurate depends entirely upon where the true value lies, unseen. State accuracy qualitatively — more or less accurate — and never as a number.
sagan.13: The resolution of an instrument is the smallest change in the measured quantity that produces a perceptible change in the reading. The resolution of a millimetre ruler is 1 mm; the resolution of a stopwatch reading to 0.01 s is 0.01 s. Resolution is a property of the scale itself, and it is not the same thing as the uncertainty of a measurement made with the instrument.
sagan.14: A measurement error is the gap between a measurement result and the true value. Uncertainty is another thing altogether: it is the interval within which the true value is expected to lie. A result written as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty, such as 20 ± 1 mm, declares that the true value is expected to lie somewhere between 19 mm and 21 mm. On a graph this interval is drawn as an uncertainty bar — a small honest confession of how much we do not know.
sagan.15: Trap Resolution is not uncertainty. The resolution of a millimetre ruler is 1 mm, but by VCE convention its measurement uncertainty is half the smallest scale increment, ±0.5 mm. To state the uncertainty as the full increment, or to confuse the two terms, is among the most common of errors.
sagan.16: Two kinds of error reach into our measurements, each in its own way.
sagan.17: Trap Averaging reduces random error only. Systematic error pushes all readings equally in the same direction, so taking more readings and averaging them will not remove it — not in a thousand trials. A systematic error is banished only by finding and correcting its cause, for example by zeroing the instrument or eliminating parallax.
sagan.18: Repeatability is the closeness of agreement between successive measurements made under the same conditions — the same observer, instrument, method and location, over a short span of time. Repeatability evaluates precision. Reproducibility is the closeness of agreement when the conditions are changed: a different observer, instrument, method or location. Reproducibility puts claimed precision to the test and can lay bare a hidden systematic error, and so it reaches toward accuracy.
sagan.19: An investigation is valid if it truly investigates what it sets out to investigate. Validity is threatened when controlled variables are allowed to drift, so that a change in the dependent variable can no longer be laid at the door of the independent variable, or by observer bias, in which the observer’s own expectations steal into the readings.
sagan.20: Key idea Random error scatters readings and harms precision; it is tamed by repeating and averaging. Systematic error shifts every reading the same way and harms accuracy; averaging cannot fix it. Repeatability and reproducibility evaluate precision; validity is the question of whether the experiment tests what it claims to test.
sagan.21: Calculated results are stated to a number of significant figures in keeping with the precision of the data — never claiming to know more than the measurements allow. Two rules govern the calculations.
sagan.22: Rounding is carried out only at the final step. Intermediate results are held to extra figures, so that the small errors of rounding cannot creep in and accumulate along the way.
sagan.23: Trap The two significant-figure rules are not interchangeable. Addition and subtraction are governed by decimal places; multiplication and division are governed by significant figures. To round at each intermediate step, rather than only at the end, is to invite an avoidable error into your work.
sagan.24: By VCE convention, the uncertainty of a reading taken with an analogue or digital instrument is half its smallest scale increment. A millimetre ruler has a smallest increment of 1 mm, giving an uncertainty of ±0.5 mm. A stopwatch reading to 0.01 s has an uncertainty of ±0.005 s. A result is reported as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty:
sagan.25: The percentage uncertainty expresses the uncertainty as a fraction of the measurement itself, which lets us weigh the relative quality of different measurements against one another:
sagan.26: A length is measured with a millimetre ruler as 152 mm. The smallest scale increment is 1 mm, so the uncertainty is half of this, ±0.5 mm.
sagan.27: Result: the length is 152.0 ± 0.5 mm. The true value is expected to lie somewhere between 151.5 mm and 152.5 mm.
sagan.28: Percentage uncertainty: 0.5152.0 × 100 = 0.33%, stated to two significant figures.
sagan.29: A trolley travels 1.27 m in 0.42 s, and the average speed is sought. The calculation is a division, so the answer keeps the fewest significant figures of the values used. The value 1.27 m carries three significant figures and 0.42 s carries two, so the answer is stated to two significant figures.
sagan.30: Speed = 1.270.42 = 3.0238… = 3.0 m s⁻¹. Rounding is applied only at this final step.
sagan.31: Key idea State every result as a mean ± uncertainty, with the uncertainty equal to half the smallest scale increment. Apply decimal places when adding or subtracting and significant figures when multiplying or dividing, and round only at the final step — never before.
sagan.32: A graph reveals the relationship between the independent and dependent variables — it lets the eye see what columns of numbers conceal. The independent variable is plotted on the horizontal axis and the dependent variable on the vertical axis. Each axis is labelled with its quantity and unit, and scaled so that the data fill the available area. Each plotted point carries uncertainty bars: a vertical bar shows the uncertainty in the dependent variable and a horizontal bar shows the uncertainty in the independent variable. The true value for each point is expected to lie within its bars.
sagan.33: When the relationship is linear, a line of best fit is drawn so that it passes as close as it can to all the points, with the points scattered evenly above and below it. The line of best fit should pass within the uncertainty bars of the points. Several temptations are to be resisted: the line must not be drawn through only the first and last points, must not be forced through a chosen point or through the origin, and must not be carried beyond the range of the data into territory you never measured.
sagan.34: Trap A line of best fit is not drawn through the first and last points only, is not forced through the origin, and is not extrapolated beyond the data. If a line that is expected on theoretical grounds to pass through the origin does not, that displacement is no accident — it is evidence of a systematic error speaking to you.
sagan.35: When the relationship between the raw variables is not a straight line, the data are linearised: one variable is transformed so that a plot of the transformed data straightens into a line. Both the variable and its axis unit must be transformed together. If a quantity is expected to depend on the square of the time, the values of time are squared and the axis is labelled with the squared quantity and its unit, for example “time² (s²)”. The gradient of the linearised graph then equals a physical quantity — a number with meaning in the world.
sagan.36: The gradient of a straight-line graph is the rise divided by the run, read from two points that lie on the line and are chosen far apart, never from points taken from the data table:
sagan.37: The units of the gradient are the units of the vertical axis divided by the units of the horizontal axis. Any ×10ⁿ scale factor printed on an axis must be carried along when reading values. The gradient is not adjusted to match a known constant — it must be allowed to speak for itself.
sagan.38: Trap Read the gradient from two widely spaced points that lie on the line of best fit, never from points in the data table that may not lie on the line. Include any ×10ⁿ axis scale factors, give the gradient its correct units (vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit), and do not adjust the gradient to force agreement with an expected value.
sagan.39: The period T of a simple pendulum is related to its length L by T = 2π√(L/g), so a plot of T against L traces a curve. Squaring gives T² = 4π²gL, which is linear in L. The values of T are squared and plotted on the vertical axis, labelled “period² (s²)”, against L in metres on the horizontal axis.
sagan.40: Two points on the line of best fit, chosen far apart, are read as (0.20 m, 0.80 s²) and (1.00 m, 4.0 s²). The gradient is 4.0 − 0.801.00 − 0.20 = 3.20.80 = 4.0 s² m⁻¹, with units of vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit.
sagan.41: The gradient equals 4π²g, so g = 4π²gradient = 39.54.0 = 9.9 m s⁻², stated to two significant figures. From the gradient of a straight line we have drawn out the acceleration due to gravity itself — a physical quantity, conjured from the slope of a graph.
sagan.42: Key idea Linearise by transforming the variable and its axis unit together, so that the gradient of the straight line equals a physical quantity. Read the gradient as rise over run from two widely spaced points on the line of best fit, give it the correct units, and include any axis scale factors.
sagan.43: Primary data are your evidence — the testimony of the world itself, bearing upon the hypothesis. Evidence is organised, analysed and evaluated in the search for patterns; the gradient of linearised data, the trend of the line of best fit, and the spread of the points relative to their uncertainty bars are all read as evidence. A conclusion states how strongly that evidence supports the hypothesis, only after the causes of uncertainty, the assumptions made, and the limitations of the data and method have each been weighed.
sagan.44: Evidence can support or refute a hypothesis, a model or a theory, but it can never establish one with certainty — this is the deep humility at the heart of science. A conclusion therefore states that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis. The words “proves” and “disproves” are never used, for no finite set of measurements, however many, can prove a general claim about the universe.
sagan.45: Trap A conclusion never states that the evidence “proves” or “disproves” the hypothesis. The right words are that the evidence supports, partially supports, or does not support the hypothesis. Evidence that partially supports a hypothesis matches the predicted trend over part of the range, or matches the trend but not the predicted value.
sagan.46: The evaluation names the limitations of the investigation, the assumptions on which it leaned, and the causes of uncertainty in the data, and then proposes improvements that would address them. A limitation is a specific weakness — a restricted range of the independent variable, perhaps, or a controlled variable that could not be held perfectly constant; an improvement is a concrete change that would lessen its effect. To see one’s own work clearly, flaws and all, is itself a scientific virtue.
sagan.47: The investigation is reported in the shared conventions of science communication: correct terminology, symbols, equations and standard abbreviations; consistent units; and results stated to an appropriate number of significant figures. Quantities are written with their units in upright form, such as m s⁻¹, and uncertainties with the ± symbol.
sagan.48: The investigation is assessed as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. At its very heart sits a single Communication statement: one sentence that answers the investigation question. This statement occupies 20–25% of the poster area — the bright centre toward which every other section points. The poster carries the prescribed sections.
sagan.49: Communication on a poster is spare and disciplined: the word limit demands that only the essential survives, and that all references and sources of assistance are acknowledged. The key findings and their implications are set out clearly enough that any reader can judge for themselves how the evidence answers the question.
sagan.50: Key idea The poster answers the investigation question in a single central Communication statement, upheld by results that reveal the trend and a discussion that weighs the evidence. The conclusion states that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis — all within a limit of 600 words and the prescribed sections.

tyson.0: Here’s the assignment: you design a practical investigation, you generate your own primary data on fields, motion or light, you pick one continuous independent variable, and the whole thing gets judged as a scientific poster. This chapter is your playbook — how to design the investigation, how to weigh the quality of every measurement, how to crunch and graph the data, and how to land a conclusion you can actually defend, written in the conventions the markers expect.
tyson.1: It all starts with a sharp investigation question: one specific, answerable question about how two measurable quantities relate. That question becomes the title of your poster — so make it count. From it everything else flows: the aim, a clean statement of what you set out to determine, and the relevant physics concepts and key definitions, pulled straight from the study design so your whole design stands on established theory, not guesswork.
tyson.2: A hypothesis is your bet, placed before you touch the data: a testable, falsifiable prediction of how the variables relate, expressed up front and backed by the background physics. And it has to commit to a direction or a form — for example, that the period of a pendulum increases with its length, or that the magnetic field strength decreases with distance from a magnet. A prediction goes one step further: it states the expected outcome of your specific procedure.
tyson.3: An investigation in this area tests how one quantity rides on another — so before you measure a single thing, you sort everything into three classes of variable.
tyson.4: Key idea You change the independent variable, you measure the dependent variable, and you nail down every controlled variable so it stays constant. With one continuous independent variable, you measure the dependent variable at a whole series of values across a range — that’s what lets you graph the relationship.
tyson.5: The methodology is the type of scientific investigation you choose; the method is the actual step-by-step procedure you carry out. For one continuous independent variable, there’s a clear winner — a controlled experiment: you vary the independent variable across a range, you hold every controlled variable constant, and you measure the dependent variable at each value. The method spells out the gritty detail: the equipment, the exact values of the independent variable you’ll test, how many repeated measurements you take at each one, and the instruments you use.
tyson.6: Run the method and you generate primary data — data you collected first-hand, yourself. It comes in two flavours: quantitative (numbers, like a time in seconds or a field strength in millitesla) or qualitative (observed properties that aren’t numbers, like the colour of a fringe or whether a surface is reflective). An investigation with a continuous independent variable runs on quantitative primary data — but log the qualitative observations too, wherever they back up your analysis.
tyson.7: Before you collect a thing, apply the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines. A risk assessment names the hazards and the controls that tame them: eye protection where projectiles or lasers are in play, safe handling of masses and electrical supplies, and limits on laser power and exposure. Ethics demands honesty — you record all your results, including the ones that snub your hypothesis, and you acknowledge every source and every bit of help you got.
tyson.8: Every scrap of primary data, every observation, every procedure, every change you make to the method — it all goes in a logbook, written down the moment it happens. The logbook is dated and kept in chronological order; it’s what authenticates the primary data as your own first-hand work, and it’s the record you’ll mine when you process and report your results later.
tyson.9: Connects The physics you’re investigating comes straight out of Units 3 and 4: a fields investigation might measure field strength against distance (topic 5, topic 7), a motion investigation might measure acceleration or period, and a light investigation might measure fringe spacing or intensity (topic 13). Whichever context you pick, the design conventions on this page apply.
tyson.10: Every measurement gets held up against the true value — the value you’d get if you could measure the quantity perfectly. You can never know it exactly. But the language of measurement quality is built precisely for this: it tells you how close your result is likely to be to that true value, and how you got there.
tyson.11: Accuracy is a qualitative call: a measurement is accurate if it sits close to the true value. You describe it as more accurate or less accurate — you never pin a number on it. Precision is a different beast: it’s how tightly your repeated measurements cluster, the spread of the readings. Precision says nothing about closeness to the true value, so it stands apart from accuracy. A set of readings can be precise but inaccurate — agreeing beautifully with each other while every last one sits far from the truth.
tyson.12: Trap Accuracy and precision are not synonyms — don’t swap one for the other. Precision is the agreement among repeated measurements; accuracy is closeness to the true value. A tightly clustered set of readings is precise; whether it’s also accurate depends entirely on where the true value sits. State accuracy qualitatively — more or less accurate — and never as a number.
tyson.13: The resolution of an instrument is the smallest change in the measured quantity that actually nudges the reading. The resolution of a millimetre ruler is 1 mm; the resolution of a stopwatch reading to 0.01 s is 0.01 s. Resolution is a property of the scale — and it is not the same thing as the uncertainty of a measurement you make with that instrument.
tyson.14: A measurement error is the gap between your result and the true value. Uncertainty is something else entirely: it’s the interval within which you expect the true value to lie. Write a result as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty — say 20 ± 1 mm — and you’re saying the true value is expected to lie between 19 mm and 21 mm. On a graph, you draw that interval as an uncertainty bar.
tyson.15: Trap Resolution is not uncertainty. The resolution of a millimetre ruler is 1 mm — but by VCE convention its measurement uncertainty is half the smallest scale increment, ±0.5 mm. Quote the uncertainty as the full increment, or muddle the two terms, and you’ve walked into one of the most common errors there is.
tyson.16: Two kinds of error go after your measurements, and they do it in completely different ways.
tyson.17: Trap Averaging reduces random error only. Systematic error hits every reading the same way, so piling up more readings and averaging them does nothing to it. You kill a systematic error by hunting down its cause and fixing it — zeroing the instrument, say, or eliminating parallax.
tyson.18: Repeatability is how closely successive measurements agree when nothing changes: same observer, same instrument, same method, same location, over a short time. Repeatability evaluates precision. Reproducibility is how closely measurements agree when you do change the conditions: a different observer, instrument, method or location. Reproducibility puts claimed precision to the test and can expose a systematic error — so it links to accuracy.
tyson.19: An investigation is valid if it investigates what it set out to investigate. Validity is under threat the moment controlled variables are allowed to drift, because then you can no longer pin the change in the dependent variable on the independent variable — or it’s threatened by observer bias, where the observer’s expectations leak into the readings.
tyson.20: Key idea Random error scatters your readings and harms precision; you beat it back by repeating and averaging. Systematic error shoves every reading the same way and harms accuracy; averaging won’t touch it. Repeatability and reproducibility evaluate precision; validity is whether the experiment tests what it claims to test.
tyson.21: You state calculated results to a number of significant figures that matches the precision of your data — no more, no less. Two rules run the show.
tyson.22: Round once, and only at the very last step. Carry intermediate results to extra figures so that rounding doesn’t pile up on itself.
tyson.23: Trap The two significant-figure rules are not interchangeable. Addition and subtraction are ruled by decimal places; multiplication and division are ruled by significant figures. And rounding at every intermediate step instead of only at the end injects error you never had to make.
tyson.24: By VCE convention, the uncertainty of a reading from an analogue or digital instrument is half its smallest scale increment. A millimetre ruler has a smallest increment of 1 mm, giving an uncertainty of ±0.5 mm. A stopwatch reading to 0.01 s has an uncertainty of ±0.005 s. You report the result as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty:
tyson.25: The percentage uncertainty recasts the uncertainty as a fraction of the measurement — and that’s what lets you compare the relative quality of measurements that are otherwise nothing alike:
tyson.26: You measure a length with a millimetre ruler and read 152 mm. The smallest scale increment is 1 mm, so the uncertainty is half of that, ±0.5 mm.
tyson.27: Result: the length is 152.0 ± 0.5 mm. The true value is expected to lie between 151.5 mm and 152.5 mm.
tyson.28: Percentage uncertainty: 0.5152.0 × 100 = 0.33%, stated to two significant figures.
tyson.29: A trolley covers 1.27 m in 0.42 s, and you need the average speed. It’s a division, so the answer keeps the fewest significant figures of the values you fed in. The value 1.27 m carries three significant figures and 0.42 s carries two — so the answer comes out to two significant figures.
tyson.30: Speed = 1.270.42 = 3.0238… = 3.0 m s⁻¹. You round only at this final step.
tyson.31: Key idea State every result as a mean ± uncertainty, with the uncertainty equal to half the smallest scale increment. Use decimal places when adding or subtracting and significant figures when multiplying or dividing — and round only at the final step.
tyson.32: A graph is where the relationship between your variables shows itself. The independent variable goes on the horizontal axis, the dependent variable on the vertical axis. Label each axis with its quantity and unit, and scale it so the data fill the available area — no wasted space. Every plotted point carries uncertainty bars: a vertical bar for the uncertainty in the dependent variable, a horizontal bar for the uncertainty in the independent variable. The true value for each point is expected to live inside its bars.
tyson.33: When the relationship is linear, you draw a line of best fit — threaded as close as possible to all the points, with the points scattered evenly above and below it. The line should pass within the uncertainty bars of the points. And there are traps to dodge: don’t draw the line through just the first and last points, don’t force it through some chosen point or through the origin, and don’t extend it past the range of your data.
tyson.34: Trap A line of best fit is not drawn through the first and last points only, is not forced through the origin, and is not extrapolated beyond the data. And here’s the payoff: if a line that should pass through the origin on theoretical grounds doesn’t, that displacement is your evidence of a systematic error.
tyson.35: When the raw variables don’t plot as a straight line, you linearise: transform one variable so the plot of the transformed data comes out straight. Transform both the variable and its axis unit — both, every time. If a quantity is expected to depend on the square of the time, you square the values of time and label the axis with the squared quantity and its unit, for example “time² (s²)”. The gradient of that linearised graph then equals a physical quantity — that’s the whole point.
tyson.36: The gradient of a straight-line graph is the rise divided by the run, read from two points that lie on the line and are chosen far apart — not from points yanked out of the data table:
tyson.37: The units of the gradient are the units of the vertical axis divided by the units of the horizontal axis. Any ×10ⁿ scale factor printed on an axis has to be folded in when you read values. And you do not adjust the gradient to match a known constant — ever.
tyson.38: Trap Read the gradient from two widely spaced points that lie on the line of best fit — never from points in the data table, which may not sit on the line at all. Fold in any ×10ⁿ axis scale factors, give the gradient its correct units (vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit), and don’t fudge the gradient to force agreement with an expected value.
tyson.39: The period T of a simple pendulum is tied to its length L by T = 2π√(L/g) — so a plot of T against L bends into a curve. Square it and you get T² = 4π²gL, which is linear in L. So you square the values of T and plot them on the vertical axis, labelled “period² (s²)”, against L in metres on the horizontal axis.
tyson.40: Two points on the line of best fit, chosen far apart, read as (0.20 m, 0.80 s²) and (1.00 m, 4.0 s²). The gradient is 4.0 − 0.801.00 − 0.20 = 3.20.80 = 4.0 s² m⁻¹, with units of vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit.
tyson.41: The gradient equals 4π²g, so g = 4π²gradient = 39.54.0 = 9.9 m s⁻², stated to two significant figures. There it is — the gradient of a linearised graph just handed you a physical quantity.
tyson.42: Key idea Linearise by transforming the variable and its axis unit together, so the gradient of the straight line equals a physical quantity. Read the gradient as rise over run from two widely spaced points on the line of best fit, give it the correct units, and fold in any axis scale factors.
tyson.43: Your primary data is evidence — evidence that bears on the hypothesis. You organise it, analyse it and evaluate it to find the patterns: the gradient of linearised data, the trend of the line of best fit, the spread of the points relative to their uncertainty bars — every one of these reads as evidence. A conclusion then states how strongly that evidence supports the hypothesis — but only after you’ve weighed the causes of uncertainty, the assumptions you made, and the limitations of your data and method.
tyson.44: Evidence can support or refute a hypothesis, model or theory — but it can never establish one with certainty. So a conclusion states that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis. The words “proves” and “disproves” never appear — because a finite pile of measurements can’t prove a general claim. Full stop.
tyson.45: Trap A conclusion never states that the evidence “proves” or “disproves” the hypothesis. The correct expressions are that the evidence supports, partially supports, or does not support the hypothesis. Evidence that partially supports a hypothesis matches the predicted trend over part of the range, or matches the trend but not the predicted value.
tyson.46: The evaluation calls out the limitations of your investigation, the assumptions it leaned on, and the causes of uncertainty in the data — then it proposes improvements that would deal with them. A limitation is a specific weakness, like a restricted range of the independent variable or a controlled variable you couldn’t hold perfectly constant; an improvement is a concrete change that would shrink its effect.
tyson.47: You report the investigation using the conventions of science communication: correct terminology, symbols, equations and standard abbreviations; consistent units; and results stated to an appropriate number of significant figures. Quantities go down with their units in upright form, like m s⁻¹, and uncertainties with the ± symbol.
tyson.48: The whole investigation is judged as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. At its heart sits a single Communication statement: one sentence that answers the investigation question. That statement claims 20–25% of the poster area — it’s the centrepiece. Around it, the poster carries the prescribed sections.
tyson.49: Communication on a poster is lean: the word limit forces you to include only what’s essential, and every reference and every source of assistance gets acknowledged. Lay out the key findings and their implications clearly enough that a reader can judge for themselves how the evidence answers the question.
tyson.50: Key idea The poster answers the investigation question in a single central Communication statement, backed by results that reveal the trend and a discussion that evaluates the evidence. The conclusion states that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis — all inside a limit of 600 words and the prescribed sections.

A student-designed practical investigation is exactly that moment: it generates primary data on fields, motion or light, using one continuous independent variable, and it is judged as a scientific poster. This chapter walks with you through how to design the investigation, how to weigh the quality of its measurements, how to process and graph the data, and how to communicate a conclusion you can defend, all in the conventions the science expects.

cox.5: It helps to keep two words apart. The methodology is the overall type of scientific investigation you have chosen; the method is the specific, step-by-step procedure you actually carry out. For an investigation with one continuous independent variable, the natural methodology is a controlled experiment: you vary the independent variable across a range while every controlled variable is held constant, and you measure the dependent variable at each value. The method, then, sets down the equipment, the values of the independent variable to be tested, the number of repeated measurements at each value, and the instruments used.
cox.6: What this method gives you is primary data, data gathered first-hand, by you. Primary data may be quantitative (numerical measurements, such as a time in seconds or a field strength in millitesla) or qualitative (observed properties that carry no number, such as the colour of a fringe or whether a surface is reflective). An investigation built on a continuous independent variable leans on quantitative primary data, with qualitative observations noted down wherever they help the analysis.
cox.7: Before any data are collected, the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines are brought to bear. A risk assessment names the hazards and the controls that reduce them: eye protection where projectiles or lasers are in use, safe handling of masses and electrical supplies, and limits on laser power and exposure. And ethical conduct asks something quietly demanding of you, the honest recording of all results, including the ones that stubbornly refuse to match your hypothesis, and the acknowledgement of every source and every helping hand.
cox.8: Everything you do is written down as it happens. All primary data, observations, procedures and changes to the method are recorded in a logbook at the very moment they occur. The logbook is dated and kept in chronological order; it is what authenticates the primary data as your own first-hand work, and it is the record from which, later, your results are processed and reported.
cox.9: Connects The physics you are investigating is drawn from Units 3 and 4: a fields investigation may measure field strength against distance (topic 5, topic 7), a motion investigation may measure acceleration or period, and a light investigation may measure fringe spacing or intensity (topic 13). Whichever context you choose, the design conventions on this page apply just the same.
cox.10: Every measurement you make is quietly held up against the true value: the value you would find if the quantity could somehow be measured perfectly. We can never know the true value exactly, and there is something humbling in that, but the language of measurement quality lets us describe how close a result is likely to be to it, and how that result came to be.
cox.11: Accuracy is a qualitative description: a measurement is accurate if it sits close to the true value. We speak of it as more accurate or less accurate; it is never stated as a number. Precision is something else entirely, how close repeated measurements are to one another, the spread of the readings. Precision says nothing about closeness to the true value, so it stands apart from accuracy. A set of readings may be beautifully precise yet inaccurate, all agreeing closely with each other while sitting, together, a long way from the truth.
cox.12: Trap Accuracy and precision are not the same word in disguise, and they must never be swapped. Precision is the agreement among repeated measurements; accuracy is closeness to the true value. A tightly clustered set of readings is precise; whether it is also accurate depends entirely on where the true value happens to lie. State accuracy qualitatively, more or less accurate, and never as a number.
cox.13: The resolution of an instrument is the smallest change in the measured quantity that produces a perceptible change in the reading. The resolution of a millimetre ruler is 1 mm; the resolution of a stopwatch reading to 0.01 s is 0.01 s. Resolution is a property of the scale itself, and it is not the same thing as the uncertainty of a measurement made with that instrument.
cox.14: A measurement error is the difference between a measurement result and the true value. Uncertainty is a different idea: it is the interval within which the true value is expected to lie. A result written as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty, such as 20 ± 1 mm, is telling you that the true value is expected to lie between 19 mm and 21 mm. On a graph, that interval is drawn as an uncertainty bar.
cox.15: Trap Resolution is not uncertainty. The resolution of a millimetre ruler is 1 mm, but by VCE convention its measurement uncertainty is half the smallest scale increment, ±0.5 mm. Stating the uncertainty as the full increment, or quietly blurring the two terms together, is a common slip.
cox.16: There are two kinds of error, and they trouble your measurements in quite different ways.
cox.17: Trap Averaging reduces random error only. Systematic error shifts every reading the same way, so taking more readings and averaging them will not budge it. A systematic error is removed only by finding and correcting its cause, by zeroing the instrument, say, or by doing away with parallax.
cox.18: Repeatability is the closeness of agreement between successive measurements made under the same conditions: the same observer, instrument, method and location, over a short time. Repeatability is a test of precision. Reproducibility is the closeness of agreement when the conditions are deliberately changed: a different observer, a different instrument, method or location. Reproducibility checks a claimed precision and can bring a systematic error into the light, so it speaks to accuracy.
cox.19: An investigation is valid if it really does investigate what it set out to investigate. Validity is put at risk if controlled variables are allowed to wander, so that the change in the dependent variable can no longer be laid at the door of the independent variable, or by observer bias, where the observer’s own expectations creep into the readings.
cox.20: Key idea Random error scatters the readings and harms precision; you tame it by repeating and averaging. Systematic error shifts every reading the same way and harms accuracy; averaging does nothing for it. Repeatability and reproducibility evaluate precision; validity is the question of whether the experiment truly tests what it claims to test.
cox.21: Calculated results are stated to a number of significant figures that honours the precision of the data behind them. Two rules govern the calculations.
cox.22: Rounding is left until the very last step. Intermediate results are kept to extra figures, so that the small errors of rounding never get the chance to pile up.
cox.23: Trap The two significant-figure rules are not interchangeable. Addition and subtraction are governed by decimal places; multiplication and division are governed by significant figures. And rounding at each intermediate step, rather than only at the end, lets in an error you could so easily have avoided.
cox.24: By VCE convention, the uncertainty of a reading taken with an analogue or digital instrument is half its smallest scale increment. A millimetre ruler has a smallest increment of 1 mm, giving an uncertainty of ±0.5 mm. A stopwatch reading to 0.01 s has an uncertainty of ±0.005 s. A result is reported as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty:
cox.25: The percentage uncertainty expresses the uncertainty as a fraction of the measurement, which lets you weigh the relative quality of quite different measurements against one another:
cox.26: A length is measured with a millimetre ruler as 152 mm. The smallest scale increment is 1 mm, so the uncertainty is half of this, ±0.5 mm.
cox.27: Result: the length is 152.0 ± 0.5 mm. The true value is expected to lie between 151.5 mm and 152.5 mm.
cox.28: Percentage uncertainty: 0.5152.0 × 100 = 0.33%, stated to two significant figures.
cox.29: A trolley travels 1.27 m in 0.42 s, and we want its average speed. The calculation is a division, so the answer keeps the fewest significant figures of the values that went into it. The value 1.27 m carries three significant figures and 0.42 s carries two, so the answer is stated to two significant figures.
cox.30: Speed = 1.270.42 = 3.0238… = 3.0 m s⁻¹. Rounding is applied only at this final step.
cox.31: Key idea State every result as a mean ± uncertainty, with the uncertainty equal to half the smallest scale increment. Apply decimal places when you add or subtract and significant figures when you multiply or divide, and round only at the very final step.
cox.32: A graph is where the relationship between the independent and dependent variables finally shows itself. The independent variable is plotted on the horizontal axis and the dependent variable on the vertical axis. Each axis is labelled with its quantity and unit, and scaled so that the data fill the available area. Each plotted point carries uncertainty bars: a vertical bar shows the uncertainty in the dependent variable and a horizontal bar shows the uncertainty in the independent variable. The true value for each point is expected to lie somewhere within its bars.
cox.33: When the relationship is linear, a line of best fit is drawn so that it passes as close as it can to all the points, with the points scattered evenly above and below it. The line of best fit should pass within the uncertainty bars of the points. And there are a few temptations to resist: the line must not be drawn through only the first and last points, must not be forced through some chosen point or through the origin, and must not be carried on beyond the range of the data.
cox.34: Trap A line of best fit is not drawn through the first and last points only, is not forced through the origin, and is not extrapolated beyond the data. And here is something rather telling: if a line that theory expects to pass through the origin does not, that small displacement is itself evidence of a systematic error.
cox.35: When the relationship between the raw variables refuses to be a straight line, we linearise the data: one variable is transformed so that a plot of the transformed data comes out straight. Both the variable and its axis unit must be transformed together. If a quantity is expected to depend on the square of the time, the values of time are squared and the axis is labelled with the squared quantity and its unit, for example “time² (s²)”. The gradient of the linearised graph then equals a physical quantity, and that is the quiet beauty of the trick.
cox.36: The gradient of a straight-line graph is the rise divided by the run, read from two points that lie on the line and are chosen far apart, never from points lifted out of the data table:
cox.37: The units of the gradient are the units of the vertical axis divided by the units of the horizontal axis. Any ×10ⁿ scale factor printed on an axis must be included when you read the values. And the gradient is not nudged to match a known constant, it is whatever the data make it.
cox.38: Trap Read the gradient from two widely spaced points that lie on the line of best fit, never from points in the data table that may not lie on it at all. Include any ×10ⁿ axis scale factors, give the gradient its correct units (vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit), and do not adjust the gradient to force agreement with an expected value.
cox.39: The period T of a simple pendulum is related to its length L by T = 2π√(L/g), so a plot of T against L is a curve. Squaring gives T² = 4π²gL, which is linear in L. The values of T are squared and plotted on the vertical axis, labelled “period² (s²)”, against L in metres on the horizontal axis.
cox.40: Two points on the line of best fit, chosen far apart, are read as (0.20 m, 0.80 s²) and (1.00 m, 4.0 s²). The gradient is 4.0 − 0.801.00 − 0.20 = 3.20.80 = 4.0 s² m⁻¹, with units of vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit.
cox.41: The gradient equals 4π²g, so g = 4π²gradient = 39.54.0 = 9.9 m s⁻², stated to two significant figures. And there it is, the gradient of a straight line has handed us a real physical quantity, the strength of gravity, drawn out of a swinging pendulum.
cox.42: Key idea Linearise by transforming the variable and its axis unit together, so that the gradient of the straight line equals a physical quantity. Read the gradient as rise over run from two widely spaced points on the line of best fit, give it the correct units, and include any axis scale factors.
cox.43: Your primary data are now evidence, evidence that bears on the hypothesis. Evidence is organised, analysed and evaluated until its patterns begin to show: the gradient of linearised data, the trend of the line of best fit, and the spread of the points relative to their uncertainty bars are all read as evidence. A conclusion states how strongly that evidence supports the hypothesis, but only after the causes of uncertainty, the assumptions made, and the limitations of the data and method have all been weighed.
cox.44: And here is something fundamental, something I think is rather wonderful about how science works. Evidence can support or refute a hypothesis, model or theory, but it can never establish one with certainty. A conclusion therefore states that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis. The words “proves” and “disproves” are not used, because no finite set of measurements, however careful, can prove a general claim about the world.
cox.45: Trap A conclusion never states that the evidence “proves” or “disproves” the hypothesis. The right expressions are that the evidence supports, partially supports, or does not support the hypothesis. Evidence that partially supports a hypothesis matches the predicted trend over part of the range, or matches the trend but not the predicted value.
cox.46: The evaluation is where you turn an honest eye on your own work. It names the limitations of the investigation, the assumptions it leaned on, and the causes of uncertainty in the data, and then proposes improvements that would answer them. A limitation is a specific weakness, such as a restricted range of the independent variable or a controlled variable that could not quite be held perfectly constant; an improvement is a concrete change that would lessen its effect.
cox.47: The investigation is reported in the conventions of science communication: correct terminology, symbols, equations and standard abbreviations; consistent units; and results stated to an appropriate number of significant figures. Quantities are written with their units in upright form, such as m s⁻¹, and uncertainties with the ± symbol.
cox.48: The whole investigation is assessed as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. At its heart sits a single Communication statement: one sentence that answers the investigation question. This statement takes up 20–25% of the poster area, and rightly so, for it is the thing everything else exists to support. Around it, the poster carries the prescribed sections.
cox.49: Communication on a poster is an exercise in saying much with little. The word limit demands that only the essential survives, and that every reference and source of assistance is acknowledged. The key findings and their implications are explained clearly enough that a reader, coming fresh, can judge for themselves how the evidence answers the question.
cox.50: Key idea The poster answers the investigation question in a single central Communication statement, supported by results that reveal the trend and a discussion that evaluates the evidence. greene.3: An investigation in this area of study asks how one quantity leans on another, so before a single measurement is taken, three kinds of variable must be named and separated.

With one continuous independent variable, the dependent variable is measured at a series of values across a range, so that a relationship can be drawn out as a graph.
greene.5: Two words sit at the heart of the design, and they are easily confused. The methodology is the overall type of scientific investigation you choose; the method is the specific step-by-step procedure you actually carry out. The method then spells out the particulars—the equipment, the values of the independent variable to be tested, how many repeated measurements you take at each value, and the instruments you use.
greene.6: What the method yields is primary data: data you gather first-hand, with your own instruments. Such data come in two kinds. They may be quantitative—numerical measurements, a time in seconds or a field strength in millitesla—or qualitative, observed properties that carry no number, such as the colour of a fringe or whether a surface is reflective. An investigation with a continuous independent variable leans on quantitative primary data, while qualitative observations are noted wherever they help you make sense of the analysis.
