hawking.7: Before any data are collected, the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines apply. A risk assessment names the hazards and the controls that reduce them: eye protection where projectiles or lasers are used, safe handling of masses and electrical supplies, limits on laser power and exposure. Ethics demands more. Every result is recorded honestly, including the ones that contradict the hypothesis, and every source and every helping hand is acknowledged.
hawking.8: Every measurement, observation, procedure and change of method is written into a logbook the moment it happens. The logbook is dated and kept in order. It authenticates the primary data as your own first-hand work, and it is the record from which results are later processed and reported.
hawking.9: Connects The physics you investigate is drawn from Units 3 and 4. A fields investigation may measure field strength against distance (topic 5, topic 7); a motion investigation may measure acceleration or period; a light investigation may measure fringe spacing or intensity (topic 13). The design conventions on this page hold whichever context you choose.
hawking.10: Every measurement is judged against the true value: what you would find if the quantity could be measured perfectly. The true value can never be known exactly. But the language of measurement quality describes how close a result is likely to lie to it, and how that result was obtained.
hawking.11: Accuracy is a qualitative judgment: a measurement is accurate if it lies close to the true value. You call it more accurate or less accurate. You never state it as a number. Precision is something else entirely — how close repeated measurements lie to each other, the spread of the readings. Precision says nothing about closeness to the true value. So it is separate from accuracy. Readings can be precise yet inaccurate: tightly clustered, and all of them far from the truth.
hawking.12: Trap Accuracy and precision are not synonyms, and they must not be swapped. Precision is the agreement among repeated measurements. Accuracy is closeness to the true value. A clustered set of readings is precise; whether it is also accurate depends entirely on where the true value lies. State accuracy qualitatively — more or less accurate — and never as a number.
hawking.13: The resolution of an instrument is the smallest change in the measured quantity that produces a perceptible change in the reading. A millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm; a stopwatch reading to 0.01 s has a resolution of 0.01 s. Resolution is a property of the scale. It is not the uncertainty of a measurement made with the instrument.
hawking.14: A measurement error is the difference between a result and the true value. Uncertainty is a different thing: the interval within which the true value is expected to lie. Write a result as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty, such as 20 ± 1 mm, and you are saying the true value is expected to lie between 19 mm and 21 mm. On a graph that interval becomes an uncertainty bar.
hawking.15: Trap Resolution is not uncertainty. The resolution of a millimetre ruler is 1 mm, but by VCE convention its measurement uncertainty is half the smallest scale increment, ±0.5 mm. Stating the uncertainty as the full increment, or confusing the two terms, is a common error.
hawking.16: Two kinds of error attack a measurement, and they do so in different ways.
hawking.17: Trap Averaging reduces random error only. Systematic error shifts every reading the same way, so taking more readings and averaging them does nothing to it. A systematic error is removed only by finding its cause and correcting it — zeroing the instrument, or eliminating parallax.
hawking.18: Repeatability is the closeness of agreement between successive measurements made under the same conditions: the same observer, instrument, method and location, over a short time. Repeatability evaluates precision. Reproducibility is the closeness of agreement when the conditions are changed: a different observer, instrument, method or location. Reproducibility tests a claim of precision and can expose systematic error, so it links to accuracy.
hawking.19: An investigation is valid if it investigates what it set out to investigate. Validity fails if controlled variables are allowed to drift, so that the change in the dependent variable can no longer be pinned to the independent variable. It fails too under observer bias, where the observer’s expectations colour the readings.
hawking.20: Key idea Random error scatters the readings and harms precision; repeat and average to reduce it. Systematic error shifts every reading the same way and harms accuracy; averaging does not touch it. Repeatability and reproducibility evaluate precision. Validity is whether the experiment tests what it claims to test.
hawking.21: Calculated results are stated to a number of significant figures consistent with the precision of the data. Two rules govern the calculation.
hawking.22: Round only at the final step. Carry intermediate results to extra figures, so that rounding does not accumulate.
hawking.23: Trap The two significant-figure rules are not interchangeable. Addition and subtraction are governed by decimal places; multiplication and division by significant figures. Rounding at each intermediate step, rather than only at the end, introduces avoidable error.
hawking.24: By VCE convention, the uncertainty of a reading taken with an analogue or digital instrument is half its smallest scale increment. A millimetre ruler has a smallest increment of 1 mm, giving an uncertainty of ±0.5 mm. A stopwatch reading to 0.01 s has an uncertainty of ±0.005 s. A result is reported as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty:
hawking.25: The percentage uncertainty states the uncertainty as a fraction of the measurement. It lets you compare the relative quality of different measurements:
hawking.26: A length is measured with a millimetre ruler as 152 mm. The smallest scale increment is 1 mm, so the uncertainty is half of this, ±0.5 mm.
hawking.27: Result: the length is 152.0 ± 0.5 mm. The true value is expected to lie between 151.5 mm and 152.5 mm.
hawking.28: Percentage uncertainty: 0.5152.0 × 100 = 0.33%, stated to two significant figures.
hawking.29: A trolley travels 1.27 m in 0.42 s, and the average speed is wanted. The calculation is a division, so the answer keeps the fewest significant figures of the values used. The value 1.27 m has three significant figures, 0.42 s has two, so the answer is stated to two significant figures.
hawking.30: Speed = 1.270.42 = 3.0238… = 3.0 m s⁻¹. Rounding is applied only at this final step.
hawking.31: Key idea State every result as a mean ± uncertainty, with the uncertainty equal to half the smallest scale increment. Apply decimal places when adding or subtracting, significant figures when multiplying or dividing, and round only at the final step.
hawking.32: A graph reveals the relationship between the variables. The independent variable goes on the horizontal axis, the dependent variable on the vertical axis. Each axis carries its quantity and unit, scaled so the data fill the available area. Each point carries uncertainty bars: a vertical bar for the uncertainty in the dependent variable, a horizontal bar for the uncertainty in the independent variable. The true value for each point is expected to lie within its bars.
hawking.33: When the relationship is linear, a line of best fit is drawn to pass as close as possible to all the points, with points scattered evenly above and below it. The line should pass within the uncertainty bars of the points. Certain errors are to be avoided. The line must not be drawn through only the first and last points. It must not be forced through a chosen point, or through the origin. And it must not be extended beyond the range of the data.
hawking.34: Trap A line of best fit is not drawn through the first and last points only, is not forced through the origin, and is not extrapolated beyond the data. If a line that theory expects to pass through the origin does not, that displacement is evidence of a systematic error.
hawking.35: When the raw variables do not give a straight line, the data are linearised: one variable is transformed so that a plot of the transformed data is straight. Both the variable and its axis unit must be transformed. If a quantity is expected to depend on the square of the time, square the values of time and label the axis with the squared quantity and its unit, for example “time² (s²)”. The gradient of the linearised graph then equals a physical quantity.
hawking.36: The gradient of a straight line is the rise divided by the run, read from two points that lie on the line and are chosen far apart — never from points in the data table:
hawking.37: The units of the gradient are the units of the vertical axis divided by the units of the horizontal axis. Any ×10ⁿ scale factor printed on an axis must be included when reading values. The gradient is not adjusted to match a known constant.
hawking.38: Trap Read the gradient from two widely spaced points that lie on the line of best fit, never from points in the data table that may not lie on the line. Include any ×10ⁿ axis scale factors, give the gradient its correct units (vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit), and do not adjust the gradient to force agreement with an expected value.
hawking.39: The period T of a simple pendulum depends on its length L through T = 2π√(L/g), so a plot of T against L is a curve. Square it, and T² = 4π²gL, which is linear in L. The values of T are squared and plotted on the vertical axis, labelled “period² (s²)”, against L in metres on the horizontal axis.
hawking.40: Two points on the line of best fit, chosen far apart, are read as (0.20 m, 0.80 s²) and (1.00 m, 4.0 s²). The gradient is 4.0 − 0.801.00 − 0.20 = 3.20.80 = 4.0 s² m⁻¹, with units of vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit.
hawking.41: The gradient equals 4π²g, so g = 4π²gradient = 39.54.0 = 9.9 m s⁻², stated to two significant figures. The gradient of the linearised graph has yielded a physical quantity.
hawking.42: Key idea Linearise by transforming the variable and its axis unit together, so that the gradient of the straight line equals a physical quantity. Read the gradient as rise over run from two widely spaced points on the line of best fit, give it the correct units, and include any axis scale factors.
hawking.43: Primary data are evidence that bears on the hypothesis. The evidence is organised, analysed and evaluated to find patterns. The gradient of linearised data, the trend of the line of best fit, the spread of the points relative to their uncertainty bars — all are read as evidence. A conclusion states how strongly that evidence supports the hypothesis, but only after the causes of uncertainty, the assumptions made, and the limitations of the data and method have been weighed.
hawking.44: Evidence can support a hypothesis, a model or a theory. It can refute one. But it can never establish one with certainty. So a conclusion states that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis. The words “proves” and “disproves” are not used, because a finite set of measurements cannot prove a general claim.
hawking.45: Trap A conclusion never states that the evidence “proves” or “disproves” the hypothesis. The correct expressions are that the evidence supports, partially supports, or does not support the hypothesis. Evidence partially supports a hypothesis when it matches the predicted trend over part of the range, or matches the trend but not the predicted value.
hawking.46: The evaluation names the limitations of the investigation, the assumptions it relied on, and the causes of uncertainty in the data, then proposes improvements that would address them. A limitation is a specific weakness — a restricted range of the independent variable, or a controlled variable that could not be held perfectly constant. An improvement is a concrete change that would reduce its effect.
hawking.47: The investigation is reported in the conventions of science communication: correct terminology, symbols, equations and standard abbreviations; consistent units; results stated to an appropriate number of significant figures. Quantities are written with their units upright, such as m s⁻¹, and uncertainties with the ± symbol.
hawking.48: The investigation is assessed as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. At its centre sits a single Communication statement: one sentence that answers the investigation question. This statement occupies 20–25% of the poster area. The poster carries the prescribed sections.
hawking.49: On a poster, communication is succinct. The word limit forces you to include only what is essential, and to acknowledge every reference and every source of assistance. The key findings and their implications are made clear enough that a reader can judge how the evidence answers the question.
hawking.50: Key idea The poster answers the investigation question in a single central Communication statement, supported by results that reveal the trend and a discussion that evaluates the evidence. The conclusion states that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis, within a limit of 600 words and the prescribed sections.

munroe.0: You design an experiment, run it yourself, and the data you get out are yours and nobody else's. It uses one continuous independent variable, and at the end you put the whole thing on a scientific poster. This chapter is the practical part: how to design the investigation, how to decide whether your measurements are any good, how to process and graph what you collected, and how to write down a conclusion that actually holds up, in the exact conventions you're expected to use.
munroe.1: Everything starts with one sentence, the investigation question: a specific question, one you can actually answer, about how two measurable things are related. That sentence becomes the title of your poster, word for word. Out of it come the aim, which just says what you're trying to find out, and the relevant physics concepts and key definitions, which you pull from the study design so that your design is built on physics that already works rather than on a hunch.
munroe.2: A hypothesis is a prediction you can test and, crucially, one that could turn out to be wrong. You write it down before you collect any data, and you back it up with the physics you already know. It has to commit to a direction or a shape: for instance, that a pendulum's period gets longer as the pendulum gets longer, or that a magnet's field gets weaker the further you go from the magnet. A prediction is the narrower thing: what you expect this exact procedure to spit out.
munroe.3: An investigation here is about how one quantity depends on another, so before you measure anything at all you sort everything into three kinds of variable.
munroe.4: Key idea You change the independent variable, you measure the dependent variable, and you keep every controlled variable nailed down so it doesn't move. Because the independent variable is continuous, you measure the dependent variable at a whole series of values across a range, which is what lets you graph the relationship instead of just getting a single point.
munroe.5: The methodology is the overall type of investigation you pick; the method is the actual step-by-step thing you do. With one continuous independent variable, the right methodology is a controlled experiment: you vary the independent variable across a range, you hold every controlled variable constant, and you measure the dependent variable at each value. The method is the specifics, the gear you use, exactly which values of the independent variable you test, how many times you repeat the measurement at each one, and which instruments you read.
munroe.6: What comes out is primary data, meaning data you collected yourself, first-hand, not something you looked up. Primary data can be quantitative (numbers, like a time in seconds or a field strength in millitesla) or qualitative (things you observed that aren't numbers, like the colour of a fringe or whether a surface is reflective). An investigation with a continuous independent variable runs on quantitative primary data, and you write down qualitative observations when they help make sense of the analysis.
munroe.7: Before you collect anything, you apply the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines. A risk assessment lists what could hurt someone and what you'll do to make that less likely: eye protection when there are projectiles or lasers around, careful handling of masses and electrical supplies, and limits on how powerful a laser is and how long anyone's exposed to it. Acting ethically means writing down all your results honestly, including the ones that don't match your hypothesis, and crediting every source and everyone who helped you.
munroe.8: Every bit of primary data, every observation, every procedure, and every change you make to the method goes into a logbook the moment it happens. You date it and keep it in the order it occurred. The logbook is what proves the primary data is genuinely your own first-hand work, and it's the record you later go back to when you process and report your results.
munroe.9: Connects The physics you're investigating comes from Units 3 and 4: a fields investigation might measure field strength against distance (topic 5, topic 7), a motion investigation might measure acceleration or period, and a light investigation might measure fringe spacing or intensity (topic 13). Whichever one you pick, the design conventions on this page still apply.
munroe.10: Every measurement gets compared against the true value: the value you'd get if you could measure the quantity perfectly. You can't actually know the true value exactly, ever, but the language of measurement quality is a way to talk about how close your result probably is to it, and how you got that result in the first place.
munroe.11: Accuracy is a description in words, not a number: a measurement is accurate if it's close to the true value. You say more accurate or less accurate; you never put a number on it. Precision is something different, it's how close your repeated measurements are to each other, the spread of the readings. Precision tells you nothing about whether you're close to the true value, which is why it's a separate thing from accuracy. A set of readings can be precise but inaccurate: they all agree beautifully with each other and they're all wrong by the same amount.
munroe.12: Trap Accuracy and precision are not the same word, and you can't swap one for the other. Precision is how well your repeated measurements agree; accuracy is how close you are to the true value. A tightly clustered set of readings is precise; whether it's also accurate depends entirely on where the true value happens to sit. Describe accuracy in words (more or less accurate), never as a number.
munroe.13: The resolution of an instrument is the smallest change in the thing you're measuring that actually shows up as a change in the reading. A millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm; a stopwatch reading to 0.01 s has a resolution of 0.01 s. Resolution is a fact about the scale itself, and it is not the same thing as the uncertainty of a measurement you take with the instrument.
munroe.14: A measurement error is the gap between your measurement result and the true value. Uncertainty is a different thing: it's the interval you expect the true value to lie inside. A result written as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty, like 20 ± 1 mm, is saying you expect the true value to be somewhere between 19 mm and 21 mm. On a graph you draw that interval as an uncertainty bar.
munroe.15: Trap Resolution is not uncertainty. A millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm, but by VCE convention the measurement uncertainty is half the smallest scale increment, ±0.5 mm. Quoting the uncertainty as the full increment, or mixing up the two words, is a mistake people make all the time.
munroe.16: There are two kinds of error, and they mess with your measurements in different ways.
munroe.17: Trap Averaging only fixes random error. Systematic error pushes every reading by the same amount, so taking more readings and averaging them does nothing to it, you just get a precise version of the same wrong number. You get rid of a systematic error by finding what's causing it and fixing that, for example by zeroing the instrument or getting rid of parallax.
munroe.18: Repeatability is how closely successive measurements agree when nothing changes: same observer, same instrument, same method, same place, over a short stretch of time. Repeatability is a test of precision. Reproducibility is how closely measurements agree when you do change things: a different observer, instrument, method, or place. Reproducibility checks whether your claimed precision is real and can expose a systematic error, so it's tied to accuracy.
munroe.19: An investigation is valid if it actually investigates the thing it set out to investigate. Validity falls apart if controlled variables are allowed to drift, because then you can't honestly say the change in the dependent variable came from the independent variable rather than from whatever else moved. It also falls apart with observer bias, where what the observer expects to see leaks into the readings.
munroe.20: Key idea Random error scatters your readings around and hurts precision; you reduce it by repeating and averaging. Systematic error shifts every reading the same way and hurts accuracy; averaging does nothing for it. Repeatability and reproducibility are about precision; validity is the separate question of whether the experiment tests what it claims to test.
munroe.21: You state calculated results to a number of significant figures that matches how precise your data actually were. Two rules run the calculations.
munroe.22: You round only once, right at the end. Through the middle of a calculation you keep extra figures, so that rounding errors don't pile up on each other.
munroe.23: Trap The two significant-figure rules are not interchangeable. Addition and subtraction go by decimal places; multiplication and division go by significant figures. And rounding at every intermediate step, instead of just once at the end, throws in error you didn't have to have.
munroe.24: By VCE convention, the uncertainty of a reading from an analogue or digital instrument is half its smallest scale increment. A millimetre ruler's smallest increment is 1 mm, so the uncertainty is ±0.5 mm. A stopwatch reading to 0.01 s has an uncertainty of ±0.005 s. You report a result as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty:
munroe.25: The percentage uncertainty writes the uncertainty as a fraction of the measurement, which is handy because it lets you compare the relative quality of measurements that are otherwise nothing alike:
munroe.26: You measure a length with a millimetre ruler and get 152 mm. The smallest scale increment is 1 mm, so the uncertainty is half of that, ±0.5 mm.
munroe.27: Result: the length is 152.0 ± 0.5 mm. You expect the true value to lie somewhere between 151.5 mm and 152.5 mm.
munroe.28: Percentage uncertainty: 0.5152.0 × 100 = 0.33%, stated to two significant figures.
munroe.29: A trolley goes 1.27 m in 0.42 s, and you want the average speed. It's a division, so the answer keeps the fewest significant figures of the numbers you put in. 1.27 m has three significant figures and 0.42 s has two, so the answer comes out to two significant figures.
munroe.30: Speed = 1.270.42 = 3.0238… = 3.0 m s⁻¹. You round only here, at this last step.
munroe.31: Key idea State every result as a mean ± uncertainty, with the uncertainty equal to half the smallest scale increment. Use decimal places when you add or subtract and significant figures when you multiply or divide, and round only once, at the very end.
munroe.32: A graph shows you the relationship between the independent and dependent variables. The independent variable goes on the horizontal axis and the dependent variable on the vertical axis. Each axis is labelled with its quantity and unit, and scaled so the data spread out and fill the space instead of bunching into one corner. Every plotted point carries uncertainty bars: a vertical bar for the uncertainty in the dependent variable and a horizontal bar for the uncertainty in the independent variable. You expect the true value for each point to sit somewhere inside its bars.
munroe.33: When the relationship is linear, you draw a line of best fit so that it gets as close as it can to all the points, with roughly as many points above the line as below it. The line of best fit should pass within the uncertainty bars of the points. There are several things you must not do: don't draw the line through just the first and last points, don't force it through some chosen point or through the origin, and don't extend it past the range of your data.
munroe.34: Trap A line of best fit is not drawn through the first and last points only, is not forced through the origin, and is not extrapolated beyond the data. And if a line that, on theoretical grounds, ought to pass through the origin doesn't, that miss is itself evidence of a systematic error.
munroe.35: When the raw variables don't plot as a straight line, you linearise the data: you transform one variable so that a plot of the transformed data comes out straight. You have to transform both the variable and its axis unit. If a quantity is expected to depend on the square of the time, you square the time values and you label the axis with the squared quantity and its unit, for example “time² (s²)”. Once it's straight, the gradient of that linearised graph equals a physical quantity.
munroe.36: The gradient of a straight-line graph is the rise divided by the run, read off two points that lie on the line and are chosen far apart, not off points pulled from the data table:
munroe.37: The units of the gradient are the units of the vertical axis divided by the units of the horizontal axis. If an axis has a ×10ⁿ scale factor printed on it, you have to include it when you read off values. You do not nudge the gradient to make it match a known constant.
munroe.38: Trap Read the gradient off two widely spaced points that lie on the line of best fit, never off points in the data table, which may not actually sit on the line. Include any ×10ⁿ axis scale factors, give the gradient its correct units (vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit), and don't fudge the gradient to force it to agree with the value you were hoping for.
munroe.39: The period T of a simple pendulum is tied to its length L by T = 2π√(L/g), so a plot of T against L is a curve. Square it and you get T² = 4π²gL, which is linear in L. So you square the T values and plot them up the vertical axis, labelled “period² (s²)”, against L in metres along the horizontal axis.
munroe.40: Two points on the line of best fit, chosen far apart, read as (0.20 m, 0.80 s²) and (1.00 m, 4.0 s²). The gradient is 4.0 − 0.801.00 − 0.20 = 3.20.80 = 4.0 s² m⁻¹, with units of vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit.
munroe.41: The gradient equals 4π²g, so g = 4π²gradient = 39.54.0 = 9.9 m s⁻², stated to two significant figures. The gradient of the linearised graph has handed you a physical quantity.
munroe.42: Key idea Linearise by transforming the variable and its axis unit together, so that the gradient of the straight line equals a physical quantity. Read the gradient as rise over run off two widely spaced points on the line of best fit, give it the correct units, and include any axis scale factors.
munroe.43: Your primary data are the evidence that bears on the hypothesis. You organise the evidence, analyse it, and evaluate it to find patterns; the gradient of the linearised data, the trend of the line of best fit, and how far the points scatter relative to their uncertainty bars are all read as evidence. A conclusion says how strongly that evidence supports the hypothesis, but only after you've thought through the causes of uncertainty, the assumptions you made, and the limitations of the data and the method.
munroe.44: Evidence can support or refute a hypothesis, model or theory, but it can never establish one for certain. So a conclusion says the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis. You don't use the words “proves” and “disproves”, because a finite pile of measurements can't prove a general claim, there's always one more measurement you didn't take.
munroe.45: Trap A conclusion never says the evidence “proves” or “disproves” the hypothesis. The right phrases are that the evidence supports, partially supports, or does not support the hypothesis. Evidence partially supports a hypothesis when it matches the predicted trend over part of the range, or matches the trend but not the predicted value.
munroe.46: The evaluation names the limitations of the investigation, the assumptions it leaned on, and the causes of uncertainty in the data, and then proposes improvements that would deal with them. A limitation is a specific weak point, like a too-narrow range of the independent variable, or a controlled variable you couldn't keep perfectly constant; an improvement is a concrete change that would shrink its effect.
munroe.47: You report the investigation using the conventions of science communication: correct terminology, symbols, equations and standard abbreviations; consistent units; and results to a sensible number of significant figures. Quantities are written with their units in upright form, like m s⁻¹, and uncertainties with the ± symbol.
munroe.48: The investigation is assessed as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. At its centre sits a single Communication statement: one sentence that answers the investigation question. That statement takes up 20–25% of the poster area. The poster carries the prescribed sections.
munroe.49: Communication on a poster is tight: the word limit means only the essential stuff gets in, and every reference and every source of help is acknowledged. You explain the key findings and what they mean clearly enough that a reader can judge for themselves how the evidence answers the question.
munroe.50: Key idea The poster answers the investigation question in a single central Communication statement, backed up by results that show the trend and a discussion that weighs the evidence. The conclusion says the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis, all inside a limit of 600 words and the prescribed sections.

bronowski.0: There is a moment in every science when a student stops reading about discovery and undertakes one. A student-designed practical investigation generates primary data on fields, motion or light, built upon a single continuous independent variable, and is judged as a scientific poster. This chapter sets out the whole of that undertaking: how to design the investigation, how to weigh the quality of its measurements, how to process and graph the data, and how to communicate a conclusion you can defend, in the conventions the discipline has agreed upon.
bronowski.1: Every investigation begins, as inquiry has always begun, with a question clearly put—an investigation question: a specific, answerable question about the relationship between two measurable quantities. That question becomes the title of the poster. From it flow the rest: the aim, a statement of what the investigation sets out to determine, and the relevant physics concepts and key definitions, drawn from the study design so that the whole design rests upon established theory rather than upon hope.
bronowski.2: A hypothesis is a testable, falsifiable prediction of the relationship between the variables, set down before any data are collected and justified by the background physics. It is no idle guess: a hypothesis must commit itself to a direction or a form—that the period of a pendulum increases with its length, say, or that the magnetic field strength decreases with distance from a magnet. A prediction, more modestly, states the expected outcome of the specific procedure to be carried out.
bronowski.3: An investigation in this area of study asks how one quantity depends upon another, and so, before a single measurement is taken, three classes of variable must be named.
bronowski.4: Key idea The investigator changes the independent variable, measures the dependent variable, and holds every controlled variable constant—this is the discipline of the controlled experiment. With one continuous independent variable, the dependent variable is measured at a series of values across a range, so that a relationship can at last be graphed.
bronowski.5: There are two words here that are easily confused, and the distinction is worth keeping. The methodology is the overall type of scientific investigation chosen; the method is the specific step-by-step procedure actually carried out. For an investigation with one continuous independent variable, the fitting methodology is a controlled experiment: the independent variable is varied across a range while all controlled variables are held constant, and the dependent variable is measured at each value. The method, in its turn, specifies the equipment, the values of the independent variable to be tested, the number of repeated measurements at each value, and the instruments used.
bronowski.6: From the method comes primary data—data collected first-hand by the investigator’s own labour. Such data may be quantitative (numerical measurements, such as a time in seconds or a field strength in millitesla) or qualitative (observed properties that carry no number, such as the colour of a fringe or whether a surface is reflective). An investigation built on a continuous independent variable leans upon quantitative primary data, with qualitative observations set down wherever they lend weight to the analysis.
bronowski.7: Before a measurement is made, the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines are brought to bear. A risk assessment names the hazards and the controls that lessen them: eye protection where projectiles or lasers are in play, safe handling of masses and electrical supplies, and limits on laser power and exposure. And there is a deeper obligation. Ethical conduct demands the honest recording of all results, including those that defy the hypothesis, and the open acknowledgement of every source and every helping hand—for science is built upon trust no less than upon measurement.
bronowski.8: All primary data, observations, procedures and changes to the method are recorded in a logbook at the very moment they occur. The logbook is dated and kept in chronological order; it stands as witness that the primary data are the investigator’s own first-hand work, and it furnishes the record from which results are afterward processed and reported.
bronowski.9: Connects The physics under investigation is drawn from the work of Units 3 and 4: a fields investigation may measure field strength against distance (topic 5, topic 7), a motion investigation may measure acceleration or period, and a light investigation may measure fringe spacing or intensity (topic 13). Whichever context is chosen, the design conventions of this page hold for it.
bronowski.10: Every measurement is held up against the true value: the value that would be found if the quantity could be measured perfectly. That true value can never be known exactly—it sits forever just beyond our reach—yet the language of measurement quality lets us describe how close a result is likely to lie to it, and how that result was won.
bronowski.11: Accuracy is a qualitative description: a measurement is accurate if it lies close to the true value. We speak of it as more accurate or less accurate; it is never stated as a number. Precision is something else entirely—how closely repeated measurements agree with one another, the spread of the readings. Precision tells us nothing about closeness to the true value, and so it stands apart from accuracy. A set of readings may be precise yet inaccurate, agreeing beautifully among themselves while every one of them sits far from the truth.
bronowski.12: Trap Accuracy and precision are not synonyms, and we must not trade one word for the other. Precision is the agreement among repeated measurements; accuracy is closeness to the true value. A tightly clustered set of readings is precise; whether it is also accurate depends entirely upon where the true value happens to lie. State accuracy qualitatively—more or less accurate—and never as a number.
bronowski.13: The resolution of an instrument is the smallest change in the measured quantity that produces a perceptible change in the reading. The resolution of a millimetre ruler is 1 mm; the resolution of a stopwatch reading to 0.01 s is 0.01 s. Resolution is a property of the scale itself, and it is not the same thing as the uncertainty of a measurement made with the instrument.
bronowski.14: A measurement error is the difference between a measurement result and the true value. Uncertainty is a different matter: it is the interval within which the true value is expected to lie. A result written as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty, such as 20 ± 1 mm, declares that the true value is expected to lie between 19 mm and 21 mm. On a graph this interval is drawn as an uncertainty bar.
bronowski.15: Trap Resolution is not uncertainty. The resolution of a millimetre ruler is 1 mm, but by VCE convention its measurement uncertainty is half the smallest scale increment, ±0.5 mm. To state the uncertainty as the full increment, or to confuse the two terms, is among the commonest of errors.
bronowski.16: Two kinds of error reach into our measurements, and each works in its own way.
bronowski.17: Trap Averaging reduces random error alone. Systematic error touches all readings equally, so taking more readings and averaging them will not banish it. A systematic error is removed only by tracking down and correcting its cause—by zeroing the instrument, say, or eliminating parallax.
bronowski.18: Repeatability is the closeness of agreement between successive measurements made under the same conditions: the same observer, instrument, method and location, over a short span of time. Repeatability puts precision to the test. Reproducibility is the closeness of agreement when the conditions are deliberately changed: a different observer, instrument, method or location. Reproducibility checks a claimed precision and can lay bare a systematic error, and so it reaches toward accuracy.
bronowski.19: An investigation is valid if it investigates what it sets out to investigate—no more and no less. Validity is endangered if controlled variables are allowed to drift, so that the change in the dependent variable can no longer be laid at the door of the independent variable; and it is endangered too by observer bias, in which the observer’s own expectations creep into the readings.
bronowski.20: Key idea Random error scatters the readings and harms precision; it is reduced by repeating and averaging. Systematic error shifts every reading the same way and harms accuracy; averaging will not mend it. Repeatability and reproducibility put precision to the test; validity asks the deeper question of whether the experiment tests what it claims to test.
bronowski.21: Calculated results are stated to a number of significant figures in keeping with the precision of the data—no figure claiming a certainty the measurement cannot support. Two rules govern the calculations.
bronowski.22: Rounding is held back until the final step. Intermediate results are carried to extra figures, so that the small errors of rounding are not permitted to accumulate along the way.
bronowski.23: Trap The two significant-figure rules are not interchangeable. Addition and subtraction are governed by decimal places; multiplication and division are governed by significant figures. To round at each intermediate step, rather than only at the end, is to invite an avoidable error.
bronowski.24: By VCE convention, the uncertainty of a reading taken with an analogue or digital instrument is half its smallest scale increment. A millimetre ruler has a smallest increment of 1 mm, giving an uncertainty of ±0.5 mm. A stopwatch reading to 0.01 s carries an uncertainty of ±0.005 s. A result is reported as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty:
bronowski.25: The percentage uncertainty expresses the uncertainty as a fraction of the measurement, and in doing so lets the relative quality of different measurements be compared on equal terms:
bronowski.26: A length is measured with a millimetre ruler as 152 mm. The smallest scale increment is 1 mm, so the uncertainty is half of this, ±0.5 mm.
bronowski.27: Result: the length is 152.0 ± 0.5 mm. The true value is expected to lie between 151.5 mm and 152.5 mm.
bronowski.28: Percentage uncertainty: 0.5152.0 × 100 = 0.33%, stated to two significant figures.
bronowski.29: A trolley travels 1.27 m in 0.42 s, and the average speed is required. The calculation is a division, so the answer keeps the fewest significant figures of the values used. The value 1.27 m carries three significant figures and 0.42 s carries two, so the answer is stated to two significant figures.
bronowski.30: Speed = 1.270.42 = 3.0238… = 3.0 m s⁻¹. Rounding is applied only at this final step.
bronowski.31: Key idea State every result as a mean ± uncertainty, with the uncertainty equal to half the smallest scale increment. Apply decimal places when adding or subtracting and significant figures when multiplying or dividing, and round only at the final step.
bronowski.32: A graph reveals the relationship between the independent and dependent variables—it makes the hidden order visible to the eye. The independent variable is plotted on the horizontal axis and the dependent variable on the vertical axis. Each axis is labelled with its quantity and unit, and scaled so that the data fill the available area. Each plotted point carries its uncertainty bars: a vertical bar showing the uncertainty in the dependent variable and a horizontal bar showing the uncertainty in the independent variable. The true value for each point is expected to lie somewhere within its bars.
bronowski.33: When the relationship is linear, a line of best fit is drawn so that it passes as close as it can to all the points, with points scattered evenly above and below it. The line of best fit should pass within the uncertainty bars of the points. Several errors are to be guarded against: the line must not be drawn through only the first and last points, must not be forced through a chosen point or through the origin, and must not be carried beyond the range of the data.
bronowski.34: Trap A line of best fit is not drawn through the first and last points only, is not forced through the origin, and is not extrapolated beyond the data. And there is a lesson hidden here: if a line that theory expects to pass through the origin does not, that very displacement is evidence of a systematic error.
bronowski.35: When the relationship between the raw variables is no straight line, the data are linearised: one variable is transformed so that a plot of the transformed data comes out straight. Both the variable and its axis unit must be transformed together. If a quantity is expected to depend on the square of the time, the values of time are squared and the axis is labelled with the squared quantity and its unit, for example “time² (s²)”. The gradient of the linearised graph then equals a physical quantity—a number with meaning in the world.
bronowski.36: The gradient of a straight-line graph is the rise divided by the run, read from two points that lie on the line and are chosen far apart, not from points lifted out of the data table:
bronowski.37: The units of the gradient are the units of the vertical axis divided by the units of the horizontal axis. Any ×10ⁿ scale factor printed on an axis must be carried through when reading values. The gradient is what it is; it is not adjusted to match a known constant.
bronowski.38: Trap Read the gradient from two widely spaced points that lie on the line of best fit, never from points in the data table that may not lie on the line at all. Include any ×10ⁿ axis scale factors, give the gradient its correct units (vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit), and do not bend the gradient to force agreement with an expected value.
bronowski.39: The period T of a simple pendulum is related to its length L by T = 2π√(L/g), so a plot of T against L is a curve. But square it, and order emerges: T² = 4π²gL, which is linear in L. The values of T are squared and plotted on the vertical axis, labelled “period² (s²)”, against L in metres on the horizontal axis.
bronowski.40: Two points on the line of best fit, chosen far apart, are read as (0.20 m, 0.80 s²) and (1.00 m, 4.0 s²). The gradient is 4.0 − 0.801.00 − 0.20 = 3.20.80 = 4.0 s² m⁻¹, with units of vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit.
bronowski.41: The gradient equals 4π²g, so g = 4π²gradient = 39.54.0 = 9.9 m s⁻², stated to two significant figures. The gradient of a line drawn on paper has yielded the acceleration due to gravity—a physical quantity drawn out of the data.
bronowski.42: Key idea Linearise by transforming the variable and its axis unit together, so that the gradient of the straight line equals a physical quantity. Read the gradient as rise over run from two widely spaced points on the line of best fit, give it the correct units, and include any axis scale factors.
bronowski.43: Primary data are evidence—evidence that bears upon the hypothesis. Evidence is organised, analysed and evaluated until its patterns show themselves; the gradient of linearised data, the trend of the line of best fit, and the spread of the points relative to their uncertainty bars are all to be read as evidence. A conclusion states how strongly that evidence supports the hypothesis, but only after the causes of uncertainty, the assumptions made, and the limitations of the data and method have been honestly reckoned with.
bronowski.44: Here we touch upon one of the abiding truths of science. Evidence can support or refute a hypothesis, a model or a theory, but it can never establish one with certainty. A conclusion therefore states that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis. The words “proves” and “disproves” have no place here, for no finite set of measurements can ever prove a general claim.
bronowski.45: Trap A conclusion never states that the evidence “proves” or “disproves” the hypothesis. The proper expressions are that the evidence supports, partially supports, or does not support it. Evidence that partially supports a hypothesis matches the predicted trend over part of the range, or matches the trend but not the predicted value.
bronowski.46: The evaluation names the limitations of the investigation, the assumptions on which it leaned, and the causes of uncertainty in the data—and then proposes improvements that would meet them. A limitation is a specific weakness, such as a restricted range of the independent variable or a controlled variable that could not be held perfectly constant; an improvement is a concrete change that would lessen its effect. To see one’s own work clearly, and to say where it falls short, is itself a part of the science.
bronowski.47: The investigation is reported in the conventions science has agreed upon: correct terminology, symbols, equations and standard abbreviations; consistent units; and results stated to an appropriate number of significant figures. Quantities are written with their units in upright form, such as m s⁻¹, and uncertainties with the ± symbol.
bronowski.48: The investigation is assessed as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. At its heart stands a single Communication statement: one sentence that answers the investigation question. This statement occupies 20–25% of the poster area. Around it the poster carries the prescribed sections.
bronowski.49: Communication on a poster is succinct: the word limit insists that only the essential material survive, and that all references and sources of assistance be acknowledged. The key findings and their implications are made plain enough that a reader can judge for themselves how the evidence answers the question—for in the end, a finding belongs to the community only once it can be understood and weighed by another mind.
bronowski.50: Key idea The poster answers the investigation question in a single central Communication statement, supported by results that reveal the trend and a discussion that evaluates the evidence. The conclusion states that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis, within a limit of 600 words and the prescribed sections.

einstein.0: Let me imagine a student setting out to question Nature directly. With apparatus of their own arranging, they gather primary data about fields, motion or light, turning a single continuous independent variable while they watch what answers come back; and in the end they pin the whole adventure to a scientific poster. In this chapter I shall walk through how one designs such an investigation, how one judges whether the measurements can be trusted, how the data are processed and drawn as graphs, and how a conclusion that will stand up to scrutiny is set down in the conventions the examiners ask for.
einstein.1: Every scientific investigation, I have always found, begins with a clearly stated investigation question: a specific, answerable question about the relationship between two measurable quantities. That question becomes the very title of the poster. From it flow the aim, a plain statement of what the investigation sets out to determine, and the relevant physics concepts and key definitions, drawn from the study design so that the whole design rests upon established theory rather than upon hope.
einstein.2: A hypothesis is a testable, falsifiable prediction of how the variables are related, written down before any data are collected and justified by the background physics. It must commit itself to a direction or a form: I might guess, for instance, that the period of a pendulum increases with its length, or that the magnetic field strength decreases as one retreats from a magnet. A prediction, by contrast, states the expected outcome of the particular procedure one is about to carry out.
einstein.3: An investigation in this area of study asks how one quantity depends upon another, and so, before a single measurement is taken, I find it wise to sort the quantities into three classes of variable.
einstein.4: Key idea The investigator changes the independent variable, measures the dependent variable, and holds every controlled variable fixed and unmoving. With one continuous independent variable, the dependent variable is measured at a whole series of values across a range, so that the relationship between them may afterward be drawn as a graph.
einstein.5: The methodology is the overall kind of scientific investigation one chooses; the method is the precise step-by-step procedure one actually performs. For an investigation with one continuous independent variable, the fitting methodology is a controlled experiment: the independent variable is varied across a range while all controlled variables are held constant, and the dependent variable is measured at each value. The method then names the equipment, the values of the independent variable to be tested, the number of repeated measurements at each value, and the instruments to be used.
einstein.6: The method yields primary data, the data the investigator gathers first-hand, with their own eyes and hands. Such data may be quantitative (numerical measurements, such as a time in seconds or a field strength in millitesla) or qualitative (observed properties that carry no number, such as the colour of a fringe or whether a surface is reflective). An investigation with a continuous independent variable leans upon quantitative primary data, with qualitative observations noted down wherever they help the analysis.
einstein.7: Before any data are collected, the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines must be brought to bear. A risk assessment names the hazards and the controls that tame them: eye protection where projectiles or lasers are about, careful handling of masses and electrical supplies, and limits on laser power and exposure. Ethical conduct, I would insist, demands the honest recording of every result, including those that stubbornly refuse to match the hypothesis, together with acknowledgement of all sources and all help received.
einstein.8: All primary data, observations, procedures and changes to the method are written into a logbook at the very moment they happen. The logbook is dated and kept in chronological order; it vouches for the primary data as the investigator's own first-hand work, and it provides the record from which results are later processed and reported.
einstein.9: Connects The physics under investigation is drawn from Units 3 and 4: a fields investigation might measure field strength against distance (topic 5, topic 7), a motion investigation might measure acceleration or period, and a light investigation might measure fringe spacing or intensity (topic 13). Whichever context one chooses, the design conventions of this page hold just the same.
einstein.10: Every measurement is held up against the true value: the value one would find if the quantity could somehow be measured perfectly. The true value can never be known exactly — this is the quiet humility of all measurement — yet the language of measurement quality lets us speak of how close a result is likely to lie to it, and of how that result was won.
einstein.11: Accuracy is a qualitative description: a measurement is accurate if it sits close to the true value. We speak of it as more accurate or less accurate; it is never set down as a number. Precision is something quite different — how closely repeated measurements huddle together, that is, the spread of the readings. Precision tells us nothing whatever about nearness to the true value, and so it stands apart from accuracy. A set of readings may be wonderfully precise yet inaccurate, agreeing closely among themselves while all of them lie far from the truth.
einstein.12: Trap Accuracy and precision are not twins, and must never be swapped one for the other — a distinction worth holding firmly in mind. Precision is the agreement among repeated measurements; accuracy is closeness to the true value. A tightly clustered set of readings is precise; whether it is also accurate depends entirely upon where the true value happens to lie. State accuracy qualitatively (more or less accurate) and never as a number.
einstein.13: The resolution of an instrument is the smallest change in the measured quantity that stirs a perceptible change in the reading. The resolution of a millimetre ruler is 1 mm; the resolution of a stopwatch reading to 0.01 s is 0.01 s. Resolution is a property of the scale itself, and is not to be confused with the uncertainty of a measurement made with the instrument.
einstein.14: A measurement error is the difference between a measurement result and the true value. Uncertainty is another thing entirely: it is the interval within which we expect the true value to lie. A result written as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty, such as 20 ± 1 mm, declares that the true value is expected to lie somewhere between 19 mm and 21 mm. On a graph this interval is drawn as an uncertainty bar.
einstein.15: Trap Resolution is not uncertainty. The resolution of a millimetre ruler is 1 mm, but by VCE convention its measurement uncertainty is half the smallest scale increment, ±0.5 mm. To state the uncertainty as the full increment, or to muddle the two terms, is a common stumble.
einstein.16: Two kinds of error trouble our measurements, and each in its own way.
einstein.17: Trap Averaging reduces random error only. A systematic error pushes every reading the same way, so however many readings one takes and averages, it remains untouched. A systematic error is banished only by finding its cause and correcting it — by zeroing the instrument, say, or by doing away with parallax.
einstein.18: Repeatability is the closeness of agreement between successive measurements made under the same conditions: the same observer, instrument, method and location, over a short stretch of time. Repeatability puts precision to the test. Reproducibility is the closeness of agreement when the conditions are changed: a different observer, a different instrument, method or location. Reproducibility cross-examines a claimed precision and can flush out systematic error, so it ties itself to accuracy.
einstein.19: An investigation is valid if it truly investigates what it sets out to investigate. Validity is endangered if the controlled variables are allowed to wander, so that the change in the dependent variable can no longer be laid at the door of the independent variable; or by observer bias, in which the observer's own expectations creep into the readings.
einstein.20: Key idea Random error scatters the readings and wounds precision; it is reduced by repeating and averaging. Systematic error shifts every reading the same way and wounds accuracy; no amount of averaging will mend it. Repeatability and reproducibility test precision; validity asks whether the experiment tests what it claims to test.
einstein.21: Calculated results are stated to a number of significant figures in keeping with the precision of the data. Two rules govern the calculations.
einstein.22: Rounding is left for the very last step. The intermediate results are carried with extra figures, so that rounding does not quietly pile up and corrupt the answer.
einstein.23: Trap The two significant-figure rules are not interchangeable. Addition and subtraction are governed by decimal places; multiplication and division by significant figures. To round at each intermediate step, rather than only at the end, is to invite an error one could easily have spared oneself.
einstein.24: By VCE convention, the uncertainty of a reading taken with an analogue or digital instrument is half its smallest scale increment. A millimetre ruler has a smallest increment of 1 mm, giving an uncertainty of ±0.5 mm. A stopwatch reading to 0.01 s carries an uncertainty of ±0.005 s. A result is reported as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty:
einstein.25: The percentage uncertainty expresses the uncertainty as a fraction of the measurement, which lets us weigh the relative quality of different measurements against one another:
einstein.26: Suppose a length is measured with a millimetre ruler as 152 mm. The smallest scale increment is 1 mm, so the uncertainty is half of this, ±0.5 mm.
einstein.27: Result: the length is 152.0 ± 0.5 mm. The true value is expected to lie between 151.5 mm and 152.5 mm.
einstein.28: Percentage uncertainty: 0.5152.0 × 100 = 0.33%, stated to two significant figures.
einstein.29: Now picture a trolley that travels 1.27 m in 0.42 s, and suppose we want its average speed. The calculation is a division, so the answer keeps the fewest significant figures of the values used. The value 1.27 m carries three significant figures and 0.42 s only two, so the answer is stated to two significant figures.
einstein.30: Speed = 1.270.42 = 3.0238… = 3.0 m s⁻¹. Rounding is applied only at this final step.
einstein.31: Key idea State every result as a mean ± uncertainty, with the uncertainty equal to half the smallest scale increment. Apply decimal places when adding or subtracting and significant figures when multiplying or dividing, and round only at the final step.
einstein.32: A graph lays bare the relationship between the independent and dependent variables. The independent variable is plotted along the horizontal axis and the dependent variable up the vertical axis. Each axis is labelled with its quantity and its unit, and scaled so that the data spread out to fill the available area. Each plotted point wears uncertainty bars: a vertical bar showing the uncertainty in the dependent variable and a horizontal bar showing the uncertainty in the independent variable. The true value for each point is expected to lie somewhere within its bars.
einstein.33: When the relationship is linear, a line of best fit is drawn so that it passes as close as it can to all the points, with the points scattered evenly above and below it. The line of best fit should pass within the uncertainty bars of the points. Several temptations are to be resisted: the line must not be run through only the first and last points, must not be forced through some chosen point or through the origin, and must not be stretched beyond the range of the data.
einstein.34: Trap A line of best fit is not drawn through the first and last points only, is not forced through the origin, and is not extrapolated beyond the data. If a line that theory leads us to expect through the origin does not in fact pass through it, that displacement is itself evidence of a systematic error.
einstein.35: When the relationship between the raw variables is no straight line, the data are linearised: one variable is transformed so that a plot of the transformed data comes out straight. Both the variable and its axis unit must be transformed together. If a quantity is expected to depend on the square of the time, the values of time are squared and the axis is labelled with the squared quantity and its unit, for example “time² (s²)”. The gradient of the linearised graph then equals a physical quantity.
einstein.36: The gradient of a straight-line graph is the rise divided by the run, read from two points that lie on the line and are chosen far apart, not from points lifted out of the data table:
einstein.37: The units of the gradient are the units of the vertical axis divided by the units of the horizontal axis. Any ×10ⁿ scale factor printed on an axis must be carried along when reading values. The gradient is not nudged to match some known constant.
einstein.38: Trap Read the gradient from two widely spaced points that lie on the line of best fit, never from points in the data table that may not sit on the line at all. Carry along any ×10ⁿ axis scale factors, give the gradient its correct units (vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit), and do not adjust the gradient to force agreement with an expected value.
einstein.39: The period T of a simple pendulum is bound to its length L by T = 2π√(L/g), so a plot of T against L bends into a curve. Squaring gives T² = 4π²gL, which is linear in L. The values of T are squared and plotted up the vertical axis, labelled “period² (s²)”, against L in metres along the horizontal axis.
einstein.40: Two points on the line of best fit, chosen far apart, are read as (0.20 m, 0.80 s²) and (1.00 m, 4.0 s²). The gradient is 4.0 − 0.801.00 − 0.20 = 3.20.80 = 4.0 s² m⁻¹, with units of vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit.
einstein.41: The gradient equals 4π²g, so g = 4π²gradient = 39.54.0 = 9.9 m s⁻², stated to two significant figures. And so the gradient of the linearised graph has handed us a physical quantity.
einstein.42: Key idea Linearise by transforming the variable and its axis unit together, so that the gradient of the straight line equals a physical quantity. Read the gradient as rise over run from two widely spaced points on the line of best fit, give it the correct units, and carry along any axis scale factors.
einstein.43: Primary data make up the evidence that bears upon the hypothesis. The evidence is organised, analysed and weighed for patterns; the gradient of linearised data, the trend of the line of best fit, and the spread of the points against their uncertainty bars are all read as evidence. A conclusion then states how strongly the evidence supports the hypothesis, once the causes of uncertainty, the assumptions made, and the limitations of the data and method have been pondered.
einstein.44: Evidence can support or refute a hypothesis, a model or a theory, but it can never establish one with certainty — and here, I confess, lies a lesson I have learned again and again. A conclusion therefore states that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis. The words “proves” and “disproves” are never used, because no finite handful of measurements can ever prove a general claim.
einstein.45: Trap A conclusion never states that the evidence “proves” or “disproves” the hypothesis. The proper expressions are that the evidence supports, partially supports, or does not support the hypothesis. Evidence that partially supports a hypothesis follows the predicted trend over part of the range, or follows the trend but misses the predicted value.
einstein.46: The evaluation names the limitations of the investigation, the assumptions it leaned upon, and the causes of uncertainty in the data, and then proposes improvements that would set them right. A limitation is some specific weakness — a restricted range of the independent variable, say, or a controlled variable that could not be held perfectly still; an improvement is a concrete change that would lessen its effect.
einstein.47: The investigation is reported in the conventions of science communication: correct terminology, symbols, equations and standard abbreviations; consistent units; and results stated to a fitting number of significant figures. Quantities are written with their units in upright form, such as m s⁻¹, and uncertainties with the ± symbol.
einstein.48: The investigation is assessed as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. At its very heart stands a single Communication statement: one sentence that answers the investigation question. This statement takes up 20–25% of the poster area. The poster carries the prescribed sections.
einstein.49: Communication on a poster must be sparing: the word limit insists that only what is essential survives, and that all references and sources of assistance are acknowledged. The key findings and what they imply are set out clearly enough that a reader can judge for themselves how the evidence answers the question.
einstein.50: Key idea The poster answers the investigation question in a single central Communication statement, supported by results that reveal the trend and a discussion that weighs the evidence. The conclusion states that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis, within a limit of 600 words and the prescribed sections.

noether.0: A student-designed practical investigation generates primary data on fields, motion or light, varying one continuous independent variable while everything else is held fixed, and is assessed as a scientific poster. The whole enterprise is an exercise in controlled change: this chapter sets out how to design the investigation so that only one thing is allowed to vary, how to judge the quality of the measurements, how to process and graph the data, and how to communicate a conclusion that the evidence can defend, in the prescribed conventions.
noether.1: Every investigation begins by fixing precisely what it asks. A clearly stated investigation question is a specific, answerable question about the relationship between two measurable quantities, and nothing more; that question becomes the title of the poster. From it everything else follows: the aim, a statement of what the investigation sets out to determine, and the relevant physics concepts and key definitions, identified from the study design so that the design rests on established theory rather than on assumption.
noether.2: A hypothesis is a testable, falsifiable prediction of the relationship between the variables, committed to before any data are collected and justified by the background physics. What distinguishes a hypothesis from a guess is that it specifies a direction or a form: that the period of a pendulum increases with its length, say, or that the magnetic field strength decreases with distance from a magnet. A prediction is narrower still — it states the expected outcome of the one specific procedure you are about to carry out.
noether.3: An investigation in this area of study asks how one quantity depends on another, and the entire logic rests on a single separation made before any measurement is taken: three classes of variable, distinguished by what you do with each.
noether.4: Key idea One thing is changed, one thing is read out, and everything else is held fixed: the investigator changes the independent variable, measures the dependent variable, and holds every controlled variable constant. With one continuous independent variable, the dependent variable is measured at a series of values across a range, so that the relationship between the two can be drawn as a graph.
noether.5: Two words sit at different levels and must not be confused. The methodology is the overall type of scientific investigation chosen; the method is the specific step-by-step procedure carried out beneath it. For an investigation with one continuous independent variable, the methodology that fits is a controlled experiment: the independent variable is varied across a range while all controlled variables are held constant, and the dependent variable is measured at each value. The method then fills in the particulars — the equipment, the values of the independent variable to be tested, the number of repeated measurements at each value, and the instruments used.
noether.6: What the method yields is primary data, data collected first-hand by the investigator. Primary data come in two kinds: quantitative (numerical measurements, such as a time in seconds or a field strength in millitesla) or qualitative (observed properties that carry no number, such as the colour of a fringe or whether a surface is reflective). An investigation built on a continuous independent variable rests on quantitative primary data, with qualitative observations recorded wherever they support the analysis.
noether.7: Before any data are collected, the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines are applied. A risk assessment names the hazards and the controls that hold them in check: eye protection where projectiles or lasers are used, safe handling of masses and electrical supplies, and limits on laser power and exposure. Ethical conduct demands honest recording of every result — including those that contradict the hypothesis, which are exactly the ones integrity is tested by — together with acknowledgement of all sources and assistance.
noether.8: All primary data, observations, procedures and changes to the method are recorded in a logbook at the moment they occur, not reconstructed afterward. The logbook is dated and kept in chronological order; it is what authenticates the primary data as the investigator’s own first-hand work, and it is the record from which results are later processed and reported.
noether.9: Connects The physics under investigation is drawn from Units 3 and 4: a fields investigation may measure field strength against distance (topic 5, topic 7), a motion investigation may measure acceleration or period, and a light investigation may measure fringe spacing or intensity (topic 13). Whichever context is chosen, the design conventions on this page hold unchanged.
noether.10: Every measurement is judged against the true value: the value that would be found if the quantity could be measured perfectly. This true value is the fixed point the whole vocabulary of measurement is built around — it can never be known exactly, but the language of measurement quality describes how close a result is likely to lie to it, and by what means the result was reached.
noether.11: Accuracy is a qualitative description: a measurement is accurate if it lies close to the true value. Accuracy is spoken of as more accurate or less accurate; it is never written as a number. Precision answers a different question entirely — how close repeated measurements lie to one another, the spread of the readings. Precision says nothing about closeness to the true value, and so stands apart from accuracy. A set of readings may be precise yet inaccurate: tightly agreed among themselves, and all alike far from the true value.
noether.12: Trap Accuracy and precision are not synonyms and must not be traded for one another. Precision is the agreement among repeated measurements; accuracy is closeness to the true value. A tightly clustered set of readings is precise; whether it is also accurate depends entirely on where the true value happens to lie. State accuracy qualitatively (more or less accurate), and never as a number.
noether.13: The resolution of an instrument is the smallest change in the measured quantity that produces a perceptible change in the reading. The resolution of a millimetre ruler is 1 mm; the resolution of a stopwatch reading to 0.01 s is 0.01 s. Resolution is a fixed property of the scale itself, and is not the same thing as the uncertainty of a measurement made with the instrument.
noether.14: A measurement error is the difference between a measurement result and the true value. noether.15: Trap Resolution is not uncertainty. The resolution of a millimetre ruler is 1 mm, but by VCE convention its measurement uncertainty is half the smallest scale increment, ±0.5 mm. noether.16: Two kinds of error act on measurements, and they differ precisely in their symmetry — in whether they treat every reading alike or scatter them apart.
noether.17: Trap Averaging reduces random error only. Systematic error shifts all readings by the same amount, so the shift survives the average untouched, however many readings are taken. A systematic error is removed not by repetition but by finding and correcting its cause — by zeroing the instrument, say, or eliminating parallax.
noether.18: Repeatability is the closeness of agreement between successive measurements made under conditions held fixed: the same observer, instrument, method and location, over a short time. Repeatability evaluates precision. Reproducibility is the closeness of agreement when those conditions are deliberately changed: a different observer, instrument, method or location. Because reproducibility tests whether the agreement survives a change of conditions, it checks claimed precision and can expose a hidden systematic error, and so it links to accuracy.
noether.19: An investigation is valid if it investigates what it set out to investigate. Validity fails the moment the controlled variables are allowed to drift, for then the change in the dependent variable can no longer be attributed to the independent variable alone; it fails also under observer bias, in which the observer’s expectations bend the readings toward what was hoped for.
noether.20: Key idea Random error scatters readings and harms precision; it is reduced by repeating and averaging. Systematic error shifts every reading the same way and harms accuracy; averaging leaves it untouched. Repeatability and reproducibility evaluate precision; validity is the separate question of whether the experiment tests what it claims to test.
noether.21: Calculated results are stated to a number of significant figures consistent with the precision of the data — no result may claim more certainty than the measurements that fed it. Two rules govern how that precision propagates through a calculation.
noether.22: Rounding is carried out at the final step alone. Intermediate results are kept to extra figures, so that the rounding does not accumulate from one step to the next.
noether.23: Trap The two significant-figure rules are not interchangeable. Addition and subtraction are governed by decimal places; multiplication and division are governed by significant figures. Rounding at each intermediate step, rather than only at the end, introduces avoidable error.

A result is reported as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty:

noether.26: A length is measured with a millimetre ruler as 152 mm. The smallest scale increment is 1 mm, so the uncertainty is half of this, ±0.5 mm.

noether.28: Percentage uncertainty: 0.5152.0 × 100 = 0.33%, stated to two significant figures.
noether.29: A trolley travels 1.27 m in 0.42 s, and the average speed is required. The calculation is a division, so the answer keeps the fewest significant figures of the values used. noether.30: Speed = 1.270.42 = 3.0238… = 3.0 m s⁻¹. Rounding is applied only at this final step.
noether.31: Key idea State every result as a mean ± uncertainty, with the uncertainty equal to half the smallest scale increment. Apply decimal places when adding or subtracting and significant figures when multiplying or dividing, and round only at the final step.
noether.32: A graph makes the relationship between the two variables visible. The independent variable is plotted on the horizontal axis and the dependent variable on the vertical axis. Each axis is labelled with its quantity and unit, and scaled so that the data fill the available area. Each plotted point carries uncertainty bars: a vertical bar shows the uncertainty in the dependent variable and a horizontal bar shows the uncertainty in the independent variable. The true value for each point is expected to lie somewhere within the rectangle its bars define.
noether.33: When the relationship is linear, a line of best fit is drawn so that it passes as close as possible to all the points at once, with points scattered evenly above and below it. The line should pass within the uncertainty bars of the points. noether.34: Trap A line of best fit is not drawn through the first and last points only, is not forced through the origin, and is not extrapolated beyond the data. If a line that theory expects to pass through the origin does not, that displacement is not a flaw in the drawing — it is evidence of a systematic error.
noether.35: When the raw variables do not fall on a straight line, the data are linearised: one variable is transformed until a plot of the transformed data is straight. The transformation must reach the axis as well as the variable — both the values and the axis unit are transformed together. If a quantity is expected to depend on the square of the time, the values of time are squared and the axis is labelled with the squared quantity and its unit, for example “time² (s²)”. Once straightened, the gradient of the linearised graph equals a physical quantity.
