mack.0: Here’s the deal: you design your own practical investigation, you go out and collect your own primary data on fields, motion or light — steering one continuous independent variable — and the whole thing gets assessed as a scientific poster. This chapter walks you through it: how to design the investigation, how to judge whether your measurements are any good, how to process and graph what you collected, and how to land a conclusion you can actually defend, all in the conventions the examiners expect.
mack.1: Every scientific investigation starts with one sharp sentence: an investigation question, a specific, answerable question about how two measurable quantities relate. That question isn’t just a starting point — it literally becomes the title of your poster. From it flow the aim, which states what you’re setting out to determine, and the relevant physics concepts and key definitions, pulled straight from the study design so that your whole design stands on established theory rather than a hunch.
mack.2: A hypothesis is a testable, falsifiable prediction of how the variables relate — and crucially, you write it down before you collect a single data point, justified by the background physics. It has to commit to a direction or a form: that the period of a pendulum increases with its length, say, or that the magnetic field strength decreases with distance from a magnet. A prediction goes one step more concrete, stating the expected outcome of your specific procedure.
mack.3: An investigation in this area of study asks how one quantity leans on another — so before you measure anything at all, you sort the world into three kinds of variable.
mack.4: Key idea You change the independent variable, you measure the dependent variable, and you pin down every controlled variable so it can’t budge. With one continuous independent variable, you measure the dependent variable at a whole series of values across a range — that’s what lets a relationship show up on a graph.
mack.5: The methodology is the overall type of scientific investigation you pick; the method is the actual step-by-step procedure you carry out. When your independent variable is one continuous quantity, the right methodology is a controlled experiment: you vary the independent variable across a range while holding every controlled variable constant, and measure the dependent variable at each value. The method then spells out the equipment, which values of the independent variable you’ll test, how many repeated measurements you’ll take at each one, and which instruments you’ll use.
mack.6: The method hands you primary data — data you collected first-hand, yourself. It comes in two flavours: quantitative (numbers, like a time in seconds or a field strength in millitesla) or qualitative (observed properties that aren’t numbers, like the colour of a fringe or whether a surface is reflective). An investigation with a continuous independent variable runs mainly on quantitative primary data, with qualitative observations jotted down wherever they back up the analysis.
mack.7: Before you collect anything, the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines come into play. A risk assessment names the hazards and the controls that tame them: eye protection where projectiles or lasers are in the mix, safe handling of masses and electrical supplies, and limits on laser power and exposure. Ethical conduct is non-negotiable too — you record all your results honestly, including the inconvenient ones that snub your hypothesis, and you acknowledge every source and every helping hand.
mack.8: Every scrap of primary data, observation, procedure and tweak to the method goes into a logbook the moment it happens. The logbook is dated and kept in chronological order; it’s what authenticates your primary data as your own first-hand work, and it’s the record you later mine to process and report your results.
mack.9: Connects The physics you’re probing comes straight out of Units 3 and 4: a fields investigation might pit field strength against distance (topic 5, topic 7), a motion investigation might chase acceleration or period, and a light investigation might measure fringe spacing or intensity (topic 13). Whichever context you choose, the design conventions on this page still apply.
mack.10: Every measurement gets held up against the true value: the value you’d find if you could measure the quantity perfectly. You can never know it exactly — but the language of measurement quality is built precisely to describe how close your result is likely to be to it, and how you got there.
mack.11: Accuracy is a qualitative call: a measurement is accurate if it sits close to the true value. You describe it as more accurate or less accurate — never as a number. Precision is a different animal: it’s how tightly repeated measurements agree with each other, the spread of the readings. Precision says nothing whatsoever about closeness to the true value, so it stands apart from accuracy. A set of readings can be beautifully precise and yet wrong: agreeing snugly with one another while all sitting miles from the true value.
mack.12: Trap Accuracy and precision are not synonyms, and swapping one for the other will cost you. Precision is the agreement among your repeated measurements; accuracy is closeness to the true value. A tightly clustered set of readings is precise — whether it’s also accurate depends entirely on where the true value happens to lie. State accuracy qualitatively (more or less accurate), never as a number.
mack.13: The resolution of an instrument is the smallest change in the measured quantity that actually nudges the reading. A millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm; a stopwatch reading to 0.01 s has a resolution of 0.01 s. Resolution is a property of the scale itself — and it is not the same thing as the uncertainty of a measurement you make with that instrument.
mack.14: A measurement error is the gap between a measurement result and the true value. Uncertainty is a different beast: it’s the interval you expect the true value to live inside. Write a result as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty — say 20 ± 1 mm — and you’re saying the true value is expected to lie between 19 mm and 21 mm. On a graph, you draw that interval as an uncertainty bar.
mack.15: Trap Resolution is not uncertainty. A millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm, but by VCE convention its measurement uncertainty is half the smallest scale increment, ±0.5 mm. Quoting the uncertainty as the full increment, or blurring the two terms together, is a classic slip.
mack.16: Two kinds of error get at your measurements, and they do it in completely different ways.
mack.17: Trap Averaging only beats down random error. Systematic error tilts every reading the same way, so piling up more readings and averaging them does precisely nothing to remove it. You kill a systematic error by hunting down its cause and fixing it — zeroing the instrument, say, or eliminating parallax.
mack.18: Repeatability is how closely successive measurements agree when nothing changes: same observer, same instrument, same method, same location, over a short stretch of time. Repeatability is a verdict on precision. Reproducibility is how closely they agree when you do change things — a different observer, instrument, method or location. Reproducibility puts claimed precision to the test and can flush out systematic error, which is why it ties back to accuracy.
mack.19: An investigation is valid if it actually investigates what it claims to investigate. Validity is under threat the moment a controlled variable is allowed to wander, because then you can no longer pin the change in the dependent variable on the independent variable — or it’s threatened by observer bias, where the observer’s expectations quietly lean on the readings.
mack.20: Key idea Random error scatters your readings and wounds precision; you reduce it by repeating and averaging. Systematic error shoves every reading the same way and wounds accuracy; averaging won’t touch it. Repeatability and reproducibility are verdicts on precision; validity is the separate question of whether the experiment tests what it claims to test.
mack.21: You state calculated results to a number of significant figures that honestly reflects the precision of your data. Two rules run the show.
mack.22: Round only at the very last step. Carry intermediate results to extra figures so that rounding errors don’t quietly pile up on you.
mack.23: Trap The two significant-figure rules are not interchangeable. Addition and subtraction are ruled by decimal places; multiplication and division are ruled by significant figures. And rounding at every intermediate step, instead of only at the end, bakes in error you could have avoided.
mack.24: By VCE convention, the uncertainty of a reading taken with an analogue or digital instrument is half its smallest scale increment. A millimetre ruler’s smallest increment is 1 mm, so its uncertainty is ±0.5 mm. A stopwatch reading to 0.01 s carries an uncertainty of ±0.005 s. And you report a result as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty:
mack.25: The percentage uncertainty recasts the uncertainty as a fraction of the measurement itself, which lets you compare the relative quality of measurements that are otherwise apples and oranges:
mack.26: Say you measure a length with a millimetre ruler and get 152 mm. The smallest scale increment is 1 mm, so the uncertainty is half of that, ±0.5 mm.
mack.27: Result: the length is 152.0 ± 0.5 mm. The true value is expected to lie between 151.5 mm and 152.5 mm.
mack.28: Percentage uncertainty: 0.5152.0 × 100 = 0.33%, stated to two significant figures.
mack.29: A trolley covers 1.27 m in 0.42 s, and you want the average speed. The calculation is a division, so the answer keeps the fewest significant figures of the values you fed in. Now 1.27 m carries three significant figures and 0.42 s only two — so the answer gets stated to two significant figures.
mack.30: Speed = 1.270.42 = 3.0238… = 3.0 m s⁻¹. The rounding happens only at this final step.
mack.31: Key idea State every result as a mean ± uncertainty, with the uncertainty equal to half the smallest scale increment. Use decimal places when you add or subtract and significant figures when you multiply or divide — and round only at the final step.
mack.32: A graph is where the relationship between your independent and dependent variables finally shows itself. The independent variable goes on the horizontal axis, the dependent variable on the vertical. Label each axis with its quantity and unit, and scale it so the data fill the available space rather than huddling in one corner. Every plotted point wears its uncertainty bars: a vertical bar for the uncertainty in the dependent variable, a horizontal bar for the uncertainty in the independent variable. The true value for each point is expected to lie somewhere within its bars.
mack.33: When the relationship is linear, you draw a line of best fit so it threads as close as it can to all the points, with points scattered evenly above and below it. The line should pass within the uncertainty bars of the points. A few temptations to resist: don’t draw the line through only the first and last points, don’t force it through some chosen point or through the origin, and don’t stretch it out beyond the range of your data.
mack.34: Trap A line of best fit is not drawn through the first and last points only, is not forced through the origin, and is not extrapolated beyond the data. And here’s a bonus: if a line that theory says should pass through the origin stubbornly doesn’t, that displacement is evidence of a systematic error.
mack.35: When the raw variables don’t plot as a straight line, you linearise: transform one variable so that a plot of the transformed data is straight. Transform both the variable and its axis unit — not one without the other. If a quantity is expected to depend on the square of the time, you square the time values and label the axis with the squared quantity and its unit, for example “time² (s²)”. The payoff: the gradient of the linearised graph then equals a physical quantity.
mack.36: The gradient of a straight-line graph is rise divided by run, read from two points that lie on the line and are chosen far apart — never lifted from the data table:
mack.37: The units of the gradient are the units of the vertical axis divided by the units of the horizontal axis. Any ×10ⁿ scale factor printed on an axis has to be folded in when you read values off it. And you do not nudge the gradient to match a known constant.
mack.38: Trap Read the gradient from two widely spaced points that lie on the line of best fit — never from points in the data table, which may not sit on the line at all. Fold in any ×10ⁿ axis scale factors, give the gradient its correct units (vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit), and don’t fudge the gradient to force agreement with the value you were hoping for.
mack.39: The period T of a simple pendulum ties to its length L through T = 2π√(L/g), so a plot of T against L bends into a curve. Square it and you get T² = 4π²gL, which is linear in L. So you square the T values and plot them up the vertical axis, labelled “period² (s²)”, against L in metres along the horizontal axis.
mack.40: Two points on the line of best fit, chosen far apart, read as (0.20 m, 0.80 s²) and (1.00 m, 4.0 s²). The gradient is 4.0 − 0.801.00 − 0.20 = 3.20.80 = 4.0 s² m⁻¹, with units of vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit.
mack.41: That gradient equals 4π²g, so g = 4π²gradient = 39.54.0 = 9.9 m s⁻², stated to two significant figures. And there it is — the gradient of a linearised graph has handed you a physical quantity straight out of a slope.
mack.42: Key idea Linearise by transforming the variable and its axis unit together, so the gradient of the straight line equals a physical quantity. Read the gradient as rise over run from two widely spaced points on the line of best fit, give it the correct units, and fold in any axis scale factors.
mack.43: Your primary data are evidence — evidence that bears on the hypothesis. You organise it, analyse it and evaluate it to tease out patterns; the gradient of linearised data, the trend the line of best fit follows, the spread of the points relative to their uncertainty bars — all of it gets read as evidence. A conclusion states how strongly that evidence supports the hypothesis, but only after you’ve reckoned with the causes of uncertainty, the assumptions you leaned on, and the limitations of the data and method.
mack.44: Evidence can support a hypothesis, model or theory, or it can refute one — but it can never nail one down with certainty. So a conclusion says the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis. The words “proves” and “disproves” stay in the drawer, because a finite pile of measurements simply cannot prove a general claim.
mack.45: Trap A conclusion never says the evidence “proves” or “disproves” the hypothesis. The right phrasing is that the evidence supports, partially supports, or does not support it. Evidence partially supports a hypothesis when it matches the predicted trend over only part of the range, or matches the trend but not the predicted value.
mack.46: The evaluation is where you name the limitations of your investigation, the assumptions it rested on, and the causes of uncertainty in the data — and then propose improvements that would actually tackle them. A limitation is a specific weakness, like a cramped range of the independent variable or a controlled variable you couldn’t quite hold steady; an improvement is a concrete change that would shrink its effect.
mack.47: You report the investigation in the conventions of science communication: correct terminology, symbols, equations and standard abbreviations; consistent units; and results stated to a sensible number of significant figures. Quantities get their units in upright form, like m s⁻¹, and uncertainties carry the ± symbol.
mack.48: The whole investigation is assessed as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. Right at its heart sits a single Communication statement: one sentence that answers the investigation question. That statement claims 20–25% of the poster area — it’s the main event. Around it, the poster carries the prescribed sections.
mack.49: On a poster, communication is ruthlessly succinct: the word limit forces you to keep only what’s essential, and every reference and source of assistance gets acknowledged. The key findings and what they mean are spelled out clearly enough that a reader can judge for themselves how the evidence answers the question.
mack.50: Key idea The poster answers the investigation question in a single central Communication statement, propped up by results that reveal the trend and a discussion that weighs the evidence. The conclusion states that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis — all inside a limit of 600 words and the prescribed sections.

levin.0: A student designs the experiment; the experiment speaks. From fields, motion or light, the investigation gathers its own primary data — one continuous independent variable turned, again and again — and the whole of it is held up on a scientific poster. This chapter is the craft of that work: how to design the investigation, how to weigh the worth of each measurement, how to process and graph what you find, and how to say, in the prescribed conventions, a conclusion you can defend.
levin.1: Every investigation opens with a question worth answering — an investigation question, specific and answerable, asking how one measurable quantity bends to another. That question becomes the title across the top of the poster. From it everything else descends: the aim, a plain statement of what you set out to find, and the physics concepts and key definitions drawn from the study design, so that the whole design stands on theory already laid down.
levin.2: A hypothesis is a prediction you commit to before the data arrive — testable, falsifiable, and earned from the background physics. It must name a direction or a form. That the period of a pendulum lengthens as the pendulum lengthens; that the magnetic field strength fades with distance from a magnet. A prediction goes one step further still: it names the outcome you expect from the particular procedure you are about to run.
levin.3: An investigation in this area of study asks how one quantity leans on another — and so, before a single measurement is taken, three kinds of variable are named.
levin.4: Key idea You change the independent variable. You measure the dependent variable. You hold every controlled variable still. With one continuous independent variable, the dependent variable is read at a series of values across a range — so that, in the end, a relationship can be drawn.
levin.5: The methodology is the kind of scientific investigation you choose; the method is the exact sequence of steps you carry out. With one continuous independent variable, the right methodology is a controlled experiment: the independent variable is swept across a range while every controlled variable is held constant, and the dependent variable is measured at each value. The method names the rest — the equipment, the values of the independent variable to be tested, how many repeated measurements at each value, and the instruments that take them.
levin.6: What the method yields is primary data — data gathered first-hand, by your own measurement. Primary data come in two kinds. Quantitative: numbers, a time in seconds, a field strength in millitesla. Qualitative: properties that carry no number, the colour of a fringe, whether a surface throws back the light. An investigation with a continuous independent variable leans on quantitative primary data, with qualitative observations set down wherever they help the analysis.
levin.7: Before any data are taken, the health, safety and ethical guidelines are brought to bear. A risk assessment names the hazards and the controls that tame them: eye protection where projectiles or lasers are in play, careful handling of masses and electrical supplies, limits on laser power and exposure. And ethics asks something quieter but no less binding — that you record every result honestly, the ones that defy the hypothesis along with the ones that obey it, and that you name every source and every hand that helped.
levin.8: Every primary datum, every observation, every procedure, every change to the method — all of it goes into a logbook at the moment it happens. Dated, kept in the order it occurred, the logbook does two things at once: it certifies the primary data as your own first-hand work, and it holds the record from which results are later processed and reported.
levin.9: Connects The physics under the microscope is drawn from Units 3 and 4. A fields investigation may measure field strength against distance (topic 5, topic 7); a motion investigation may measure acceleration or period; a light investigation may measure fringe spacing or intensity (topic 13). Whichever context you choose, the design conventions on this page hold.
levin.10: Every measurement is held up against the true value — the value you would find if the quantity could be measured perfectly. That true value can never be known exactly. But the language of measurement quality describes the next best thing: how close your result is likely to lie to it, and how that result came to be.
levin.11: Accuracy is a word, not a number. A measurement is accurate if it sits close to the true value — more accurate, less accurate, never a quantity. Precision is something else entirely: how closely repeated measurements cluster, the spread of the readings among themselves. Precision says nothing about closeness to the truth, and so it stands apart from accuracy. A set of readings may agree beautifully with one another and still, all together, miss the true value by a mile — precise, yet inaccurate.
levin.12: Trap Accuracy and precision are not the same word wearing two coats, and they must never trade places. Precision is the agreement among repeated measurements; accuracy is closeness to the true value. A tight cluster of readings is precise — whether it is also accurate depends entirely on where the true value lies. State accuracy in words, more or less accurate, and never as a number.
levin.13: The resolution of an instrument is the smallest change in the measured quantity that the reading will register at all. For a millimetre ruler, the resolution is 1 mm; for a stopwatch reading to 0.01 s, it is 0.01 s. Resolution belongs to the scale itself — it is not the uncertainty of a measurement made with the instrument.
levin.14: A measurement error is the gap between a result and the true value. Uncertainty is a different creature: it is the interval within which the true value is expected to lie. A result written as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty — 20 ± 1 mm — declares that the true value is expected to fall between 19 mm and 21 mm. On a graph, that interval is drawn out as an uncertainty bar.
levin.15: Trap Resolution is not uncertainty. A millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm, but by VCE convention its measurement uncertainty is half the smallest scale increment, ±0.5 mm. To state the uncertainty as the full increment, or to let the two terms blur into one, is a common error.
levin.16: Two kinds of error trouble a measurement, and each in its own way.
levin.17: Trap Averaging reduces random error only. Systematic error leans on every reading the same way, so more readings and a mean will not shift it. A systematic error yields to one thing only: finding its cause and correcting it — zeroing the instrument, banishing parallax.
levin.18: Repeatability is the agreement between successive measurements made under the same conditions — same observer, same instrument, same method, same place, over a short stretch of time. Repeatability is a verdict on precision. Reproducibility is agreement when the conditions are deliberately changed: a different observer, instrument, method or location. Reproducibility tests a claimed precision and can flush out a systematic error — and so it reaches toward accuracy.
levin.19: An investigation is valid if it investigates what it claims to investigate. Validity is put at risk when controlled variables are let loose, so that a change in the dependent variable can no longer be laid at the door of the independent variable — or by observer bias, when what the observer expects to see colours what the observer reads.
levin.20: Key idea Random error scatters the readings and wounds precision; repeating and averaging draw them back together. Systematic error shifts every reading the same way and wounds accuracy; averaging never mends it. Repeatability and reproducibility judge precision; validity asks whether the experiment tests the very thing it claims to test.
levin.21: Calculated results are stated to a number of significant figures the data can honestly bear. Two rules govern the arithmetic.
levin.22: Round only at the very end. Intermediate results are carried with extra figures, so that rounding does not gather and compound as you go.
levin.23: Trap The two significant-figure rules do not stand in for each other. Addition and subtraction answer to decimal places; multiplication and division answer to significant figures. And rounding at each step along the way, rather than once at the end, breeds error that need never have been.
levin.24: By VCE convention, the uncertainty of a reading taken with an analogue or digital instrument is half its smallest scale increment. A millimetre ruler, smallest increment 1 mm, carries an uncertainty of ±0.5 mm. A stopwatch reading to 0.01 s carries an uncertainty of ±0.005 s. And a result is reported as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty:
levin.25: The percentage uncertainty casts the uncertainty as a fraction of the measurement itself — and in doing so lets the relative quality of different measurements be set side by side and compared:
levin.26: A length is measured with a millimetre ruler as 152 mm. The smallest scale increment is 1 mm, so the uncertainty is half of that — ±0.5 mm.
levin.27: Result: the length is 152.0 ± 0.5 mm. The true value is expected to lie between 151.5 mm and 152.5 mm.
levin.28: Percentage uncertainty: 0.5152.0 × 100 = 0.33%, stated to two significant figures.
levin.29: A trolley travels 1.27 m in 0.42 s, and the average speed is wanted. The calculation is a division, so the answer keeps the fewest significant figures of the values that went into it. The value 1.27 m carries three significant figures and 0.42 s carries two, so the answer is stated to two significant figures.
levin.30: Speed = 1.270.42 = 3.0238… = 3.0 m s⁻¹. Rounding is held back until this final step.
levin.31: Key idea State every result as a mean ± uncertainty, the uncertainty equal to half the smallest scale increment. Reach for decimal places when adding or subtracting, for significant figures when multiplying or dividing — and round only at the final step.
levin.32: A graph lays bare the relationship between the independent and dependent variables. The independent variable goes on the horizontal axis, the dependent variable on the vertical. Each axis is labelled with its quantity and its unit, and scaled so the data fill the space they are given. Each plotted point carries uncertainty bars — a vertical bar for the uncertainty in the dependent variable, a horizontal bar for the uncertainty in the independent variable. The true value for each point is expected to lie somewhere within its bars.
levin.33: When the relationship runs straight, a line of best fit is drawn to pass as close as it can to all the points, with points scattered evenly above and below. The line should pass within the uncertainty bars of the points. And there are errors to refuse: the line is not drawn through only the first and last points, not forced through some chosen point or through the origin, and not carried beyond the range of the data.
levin.34: Trap A line of best fit is not drawn through the first and last points alone, is not forced through the origin, and is not extrapolated past the data. And if a line that theory says should pass through the origin does not, that displacement is itself the evidence — the signature of a systematic error.
levin.35: When the raw variables do not fall on a straight line, the data are linearised: one variable is transformed until a plot of the transformed data runs straight. Transform the variable and its axis unit together — both, or neither. If a quantity is expected to depend on the square of the time, square the values of time and label the axis with the squared quantity and its unit, “time² (s²)”. The gradient of the linearised graph then comes to equal a physical quantity.
levin.36: The gradient of a straight-line graph is the rise divided by the run, read from two points that lie on the line and are chosen far apart — never from points lifted out of the data table:
levin.37: The units of the gradient are the units of the vertical axis divided by the units of the horizontal axis. Any ×10ⁿ scale factor printed on an axis must be carried into the reading. And the gradient is left as it falls — never nudged to match a known constant.
levin.38: Trap Read the gradient from two widely spaced points that lie on the line of best fit, never from points in the data table that may not lie on it at all. Carry any ×10ⁿ axis scale factors, give the gradient its true units — vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit — and do not adjust it to force agreement with an expected value.
levin.39: The period T of a simple pendulum is tied to its length L by T = 2π√(L/g), so a plot of T against L bends into a curve. Square it, and T² = 4π²gL — straight now, linear in L. The values of T are squared and plotted on the vertical axis, labelled “period² (s²)”, against L in metres on the horizontal axis.
levin.40: Two points on the line of best fit, chosen far apart, are read as (0.20 m, 0.80 s²) and (1.00 m, 4.0 s²). The gradient is 4.0 − 0.801.00 − 0.20 = 3.20.80 = 4.0 s² m⁻¹, its units the vertical-axis unit divided by the horizontal-axis unit.
levin.41: The gradient equals 4π²g, so g = 4π²gradient = 39.54.0 = 9.9 m s⁻², stated to two significant figures. The slope of a straightened graph has given up a physical quantity.
levin.42: Key idea Linearise by transforming the variable and its axis unit together, so the gradient of the straight line comes to equal a physical quantity. Read that gradient as rise over run from two widely spaced points on the line of best fit, give it its correct units, and carry any axis scale factors.
levin.43: Primary data are evidence bearing on the hypothesis. The evidence is organised, analysed and weighed for its patterns — the gradient of linearised data, the trend of the line of best fit, the spread of the points against their uncertainty bars, all of it read as evidence. A conclusion then says how strongly that evidence supports the hypothesis — but only after the causes of uncertainty, the assumptions made, and the limitations of the data and method have each been reckoned with.
levin.44: Evidence can support a hypothesis, a model, a theory; evidence can refute one. What evidence cannot do is settle the matter with certainty. So a conclusion says that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis. The words “proves” and “disproves” are never spoken — because a finite handful of measurements cannot prove a claim that reaches beyond them.
levin.45: Trap A conclusion never says the evidence “proves” or “disproves” the hypothesis. The true expressions are that the evidence supports, partially supports, or does not support it. Evidence partially supports a hypothesis when it follows the predicted trend over part of the range, or follows the trend but not the predicted value.
levin.46: The evaluation names the limitations of the investigation, the assumptions it leaned on, and the causes of uncertainty in the data — and then proposes improvements to answer them. A limitation is a specific weakness: a range of the independent variable too narrow, a controlled variable that would not hold perfectly still. An improvement is a concrete change that would lessen its effect.
levin.47: The investigation is reported in the conventions of science communication — correct terminology, symbols, equations and standard abbreviations; consistent units; results stated to a fitting number of significant figures. Quantities are written with their units upright, m s⁻¹, and uncertainties wear the ± symbol.
levin.48: The investigation is assessed as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. At its heart stands a single Communication statement: one sentence that answers the investigation question. That statement takes up 20–25% of the poster area. Around it, the poster carries the prescribed sections.
levin.49: On a poster, communication is spare. The word limit insists that only what is essential survives — and that every reference, every source of help, is acknowledged. The key findings and what they mean are made plain enough that a reader can judge, for themselves, how the evidence answers the question.
levin.50: Key idea The poster answers the investigation question in a single central Communication statement, held up by results that reveal the trend and a discussion that weighs the evidence. The conclusion says the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis — all within 600 words and the prescribed sections.

czerski.0: Here you get to be the one asking the question. You design a practical investigation that produces its own fresh data — primary data on fields, motion or light — built around one continuous independent variable, and the whole thing is judged as a scientific poster. This chapter walks you through it: how to design the investigation, how to weigh up whether your measurements are any good, how to process and graph what you collect, and how to land a conclusion you can actually defend, written in the conventions the examiners expect.
czerski.1: Everything starts with a sharp investigation question: a specific, answerable question about how one measurable quantity is tied to another. Get this right and it does double duty — it becomes the title of your poster. From that question everything else unfolds: the aim, a plain statement of what you’re setting out to determine, and the physics concepts and key definitions you pull from the study design, so that your design stands on solid, established theory rather than a hunch.
czerski.2: A hypothesis is a testable, falsifiable prediction about how the variables relate — and you commit to it before a single measurement is taken, backed by the physics you already know. It has to point somewhere: it names a direction or a form. The period of a pendulum increases with its length, say, or the magnetic field strength decreases as you move away from a magnet. A prediction goes one step further, stating exactly what you expect your particular procedure to produce.
czerski.3: An investigation in this area of study probes how one quantity rides on another — so before you measure anything at all, you sort your quantities into three kinds of variable.
czerski.4: Key idea You change the independent variable, you measure the dependent variable, and you pin every controlled variable firmly in place. Because your independent variable is continuous, you measure the dependent variable at a whole series of values spread across a range — enough points to draw a relationship out into the open as a graph.
czerski.5: The methodology is the overall kind of scientific investigation you’ve picked; the method is the actual step-by-step recipe you follow at the bench. With one continuous independent variable, the methodology that fits is a controlled experiment: you push the independent variable across a range while holding every controlled variable steady, and you measure the dependent variable at each setting. The method then spells out the nuts and bolts — the equipment, the exact values of the independent variable you’ll test, how many repeated measurements you’ll take at each one, and the instruments you’ll reach for.
czerski.6: The method hands you primary data — data you gathered yourself, first-hand, with your own hands and eyes. It comes in two flavours. Quantitative data are numbers: a time in seconds, a field strength in millitesla. Qualitative data are observed properties that aren’t numerical at all — the colour of a fringe, or whether a surface throws light back at you. An investigation with a continuous independent variable leans on quantitative primary data, with the qualitative observations jotted down wherever they help make sense of what you’re seeing.
czerski.7: Before you collect a scrap of data, you bring in the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines. A risk assessment names the hazards and the controls that tame them: eye protection wherever something can fly off or a laser is in play, careful handling of masses and electrical supplies, and firm limits on laser power and exposure. Ethical conduct means recording every result honestly — including the awkward ones that snub your hypothesis — and giving credit to every source and every helping hand.
czerski.8: Every scrap of primary data, every observation, every step of the procedure, every change you make to the method — it all goes into a logbook the moment it happens. The logbook is dated and kept in the order things occurred; it’s what proves the primary data is genuinely your own first-hand work, and it’s the record you later mine to process and report your results.
czerski.9: Connects The physics you’re investigating comes straight out of Units 3 and 4. A fields investigation might track field strength against distance (topic 5, topic 7); a motion investigation might chase acceleration or period; a light investigation might measure fringe spacing or intensity (topic 13). Whichever context you choose, the design conventions on this page still apply.
czerski.10: Every measurement is held up against the true value: the value you’d land on if you could measure the quantity flawlessly. You can never know that true value exactly — but the language of measurement quality lets you describe how close your result is likely to be, and how you arrived at it.
czerski.11: Accuracy is a word, not a number: a measurement is accurate if it sits close to the true value. You describe it as more accurate or less accurate — never as a figure. Precision is a different beast: it’s how tightly your repeated measurements huddle together, the spread of the readings. Precision says nothing about how close you are to the true value, so it’s a separate question from accuracy. A set of readings can be beautifully precise yet wildly inaccurate — all agreeing closely with one another while sitting, as a pack, a long way from the truth.
czerski.12: Trap Accuracy and precision are not the same word in different clothes, and you mustn’t swap them. Precision is how well your repeated measurements agree with each other; accuracy is how close they are to the true value. A tightly clustered set of readings is precise; whether it’s also accurate depends entirely on where the true value happens to lie. State accuracy in words (more or less accurate) and never as a number.
czerski.13: The resolution of an instrument is the smallest change in the quantity that nudges the reading at all — the finest difference it can notice. A millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm; a stopwatch reading to 0.01 s has a resolution of 0.01 s. Resolution is just a property of the scale, and it’s not the same thing as the uncertainty of a measurement you make with the instrument.
czerski.14: A measurement error is the gap between your result and the true value. Uncertainty is a different idea altogether: it’s the interval you expect the true value to live inside. Write a result as a mean give-or-take an uncertainty — 20 ± 1 mm — and you’re saying the true value is expected to sit somewhere between 19 mm and 21 mm. On a graph, that interval is drawn as an uncertainty bar.
czerski.15: Trap Resolution is not uncertainty. A millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm, but by VCE convention its measurement uncertainty is half the smallest scale increment, ±0.5 mm. Quoting the whole increment as the uncertainty, or muddling the two terms together, is a common slip.
czerski.16: Two kinds of error get at your measurements, and they do their mischief in completely different ways.
czerski.17: Trap Averaging only tidies up random error. Systematic error tugs every reading the same way, so piling up more readings and averaging them does nothing to shift it. You only get rid of a systematic error by hunting down its cause and fixing it — zeroing the instrument, say, or killing off parallax.
czerski.18: Repeatability is how closely successive measurements agree when nothing changes: the same observer, instrument, method and location, all within a short stretch of time. Repeatability is a test of precision. Reproducibility is how closely measurements agree when you do change things: a different observer, a different instrument, a different method or location. Reproducibility puts a claimed precision to the test and can flush out systematic error, so it ties back to accuracy.
czerski.19: An investigation is valid if it actually investigates what it set out to investigate. Validity is under threat the moment a controlled variable is allowed to drift — because then you can no longer pin the change in the dependent variable on the independent variable — or when observer bias creeps in and the observer’s expectations start colouring the readings.
czerski.20: Key idea Random error scatters your readings and bruises precision; you rein it in by repeating and averaging. Systematic error shoves every reading the same way and bruises accuracy; averaging won’t touch it. Repeatability and reproducibility test precision; validity is the question of whether your experiment really tests what it claims to.
czerski.21: Calculated results are quoted to a number of significant figures that honestly reflects the precision of the data behind them. Two rules run the show.
czerski.22: You round only at the very last step. Carry intermediate results to a few extra figures along the way, so that rounding errors don’t quietly pile up on top of each other.
czerski.23: Trap The two significant-figure rules are not interchangeable. Addition and subtraction go by decimal places; multiplication and division go by significant figures. And rounding at every intermediate step, instead of saving it for the end, sows error you could have avoided.
czerski.24: By VCE convention, the uncertainty of a reading taken with an analogue or digital instrument is half its smallest scale increment. A millimetre ruler has a smallest increment of 1 mm, giving an uncertainty of ±0.5 mm. A stopwatch reading to 0.01 s has an uncertainty of ±0.005 s. You report a result as a mean give-or-take an uncertainty:
czerski.25: The percentage uncertainty recasts the uncertainty as a fraction of the measurement itself, which lets you compare the relative quality of measurements that are otherwise nothing alike:
czerski.26: Say you measure a length with a millimetre ruler and get 152 mm. The smallest scale increment is 1 mm, so the uncertainty is half of that, ±0.5 mm.
czerski.27: Result: the length is 152.0 ± 0.5 mm. The true value is expected to lie between 151.5 mm and 152.5 mm.
czerski.28: Percentage uncertainty: 0.5152.0 × 100 = 0.33%, stated to two significant figures.
czerski.29: A trolley runs 1.27 m in 0.42 s, and you want its average speed. The calculation is a division, so the answer keeps the fewest significant figures of the values you fed in. Here 1.27 m carries three significant figures and 0.42 s carries two, so the answer is stated to two significant figures.
czerski.30: Speed = 1.270.42 = 3.0238… = 3.0 m s⁻¹. You round only here, at this final step.
czerski.31: Key idea State every result as a mean ± uncertainty, with the uncertainty equal to half the smallest scale increment. Reach for decimal places when adding or subtracting and significant figures when multiplying or dividing, and save the rounding for the very last step.
czerski.32: A graph drags the relationship between your two variables out where you can see it. The independent variable goes on the horizontal axis, the dependent variable on the vertical. Each axis is labelled with its quantity and unit, and scaled so the data spread out to fill the available area rather than huddling in a corner. Every plotted point carries its own uncertainty bars: a vertical bar for the uncertainty in the dependent variable and a horizontal bar for the uncertainty in the independent variable. The true value for each point is expected to lie somewhere within its bars.
czerski.33: When the relationship comes out linear, you draw a line of best fit so it threads as close as it can to all the points, with roughly as many points scattered above it as below. The line should pass within the uncertainty bars of the points. There are a few traps to dodge: don’t run the line through just the first and last points, don’t force it through some chosen point or through the origin, and don’t stretch it out beyond where your data actually go.
czerski.34: Trap A line of best fit is not drawn through the first and last points only, is not forced through the origin, and is not extrapolated beyond the data. And if a line that theory says should pass through the origin stubbornly misses it, that offset is itself a clue — evidence of a systematic error.
czerski.35: When the raw variables don’t fall on a straight line, you linearise: you transform one variable so that a plot of the transformed data comes out straight. You have to transform both the variable and its axis unit together. If a quantity is expected to depend on the square of the time, you square the time values and label the axis with the squared quantity and its unit — “time² (s²)”. The gradient of that linearised graph then hands you a physical quantity.
czerski.36: The gradient of a straight-line graph is the rise divided by the run, read off from two points that genuinely lie on the line and are chosen as far apart as you can manage — never from points lifted straight out of the data table:
czerski.37: The units of the gradient are the units of the vertical axis divided by the units of the horizontal axis. Any ×10ⁿ scale factor printed on an axis has to be folded back in when you read values off. And you never nudge the gradient to make it match a constant you were hoping for.
czerski.38: Trap Read the gradient from two widely spaced points that lie on the line of best fit, never from points in the data table that may not sit on the line at all. Fold in any ×10ⁿ axis scale factors, give the gradient its proper units (vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit), and don’t massage the gradient to force agreement with the value you expected.
czerski.39: The period T of a simple pendulum is tied to its length L by T = 2π√(L/g), so a plot of T against L curves. Square it and you get T² = 4π²gL, which is now linear in L. So you square the values of T and plot them up the vertical axis, labelled “period² (s²)”, against L in metres along the horizontal axis.
czerski.40: Two points on the line of best fit, picked far apart, read as (0.20 m, 0.80 s²) and (1.00 m, 4.0 s²). The gradient is 4.0 − 0.801.00 − 0.20 = 3.20.80 = 4.0 s² m⁻¹, with units of vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit.
czerski.41: That gradient equals 4π²g, so g = 4π²gradient = 39.54.0 = 9.9 m s⁻², stated to two significant figures. The gradient of a linearised graph has quietly handed you a real physical quantity — the strength of gravity itself.
czerski.42: Key idea Linearise by transforming the variable and its axis unit hand in hand, so the gradient of the straight line equals a physical quantity. Read the gradient as rise over run from two widely spaced points on the line of best fit, give it the right units, and fold in any axis scale factors.
czerski.43: Your primary data are evidence that bears on the hypothesis. You organise that evidence, analyse it and weigh it up to find the patterns hiding inside: the gradient of linearised data, the trend the line of best fit traces out, and how tightly the points hug the line relative to their uncertainty bars — all of it reads as evidence. A conclusion then states how strongly that evidence supports the hypothesis, but only after you’ve reckoned with the causes of uncertainty, the assumptions you leaned on, and the limitations of your data and method.
czerski.44: Evidence can support a hypothesis, model or theory, or it can refute one — but it can never nail one down with certainty. So a conclusion says that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis. The words “proves” and “disproves” stay out of it, because no finite pile of measurements can ever prove a claim meant to hold in general.
czerski.45: Trap A conclusion never says the evidence “proves” or “disproves” the hypothesis. The right phrasings are that the evidence supports, partially supports, or does not support it. Evidence that partially supports a hypothesis follows the predicted trend across part of the range, or follows the trend but lands off the predicted value.
czerski.46: The evaluation names the limitations of your investigation, the assumptions it rested on, and the causes of uncertainty in the data — and then proposes improvements that would tackle them. A limitation is a specific weak spot: a cramped range of the independent variable, say, or a controlled variable you couldn’t quite hold perfectly still. An improvement is a concrete change that would soften its effect.
czerski.47: You report the investigation in the conventions of science communication: the correct terminology, symbols, equations and standard abbreviations; units kept consistent throughout; and results quoted to a sensible number of significant figures. Quantities are written with their units upright, like m s⁻¹, and uncertainties carry the ± symbol.
czerski.48: The whole investigation is assessed as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. Right at its heart sits a single Communication statement: one sentence that answers the investigation question outright. That statement takes up 20–25% of the poster area. Around it, the poster carries the prescribed sections.
czerski.49: Communication on a poster has to be lean: the word limit forces you to keep only what’s essential, and to acknowledge every reference and every source of help. The key findings and what they mean are spelled out clearly enough that a reader can judge for themselves how the evidence answers the question.
czerski.50: Key idea The poster answers the investigation question in a single central Communication statement, propped up by results that lay the trend bare and a discussion that weighs the evidence. The conclusion states that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis — all within a limit of 600 words and the prescribed sections.

veritasium.0: Here's the thing about a practical investigation: nobody hands you the answer. You design it yourself. You generate primary data first-hand — on fields, motion or light — built around one continuous independent variable, and in the end it all gets squeezed onto a single scientific poster. This chapter walks you through the whole arc: how to design the investigation, how to judge whether your measurements are any good, how to process and graph what you collected, and how to land a conclusion you can actually defend — in exactly the conventions VCAA expects.
veritasium.1: You might think an investigation starts with the experiment. It doesn't — it starts with a single, sharply worded investigation question: a specific, answerable question about the relationship between two measurable quantities. That question isn't just a starting point; it literally becomes the title of your poster. Everything flows from it — the aim, which states what the investigation sets out to determine, and the relevant physics concepts and key definitions, pulled from the study design so that your design rests on theory that's already established rather than on a hunch.
veritasium.2: Now, a hypothesis is not just a guess about what you'll see. It's a testable, falsifiable prediction of the relationship between the variables — written down before you collect a single data point, and justified by the background physics. And here's the part students often skip: it has to commit to a direction or a form. Not “the pendulum will do something,” but that the period of a pendulum increases with its length, or that the magnetic field strength decreases with distance from a magnet. A prediction is the narrower cousin — it states the expected outcome of your specific procedure.
veritasium.3: Every investigation in this area of study is really asking one thing: how does one quantity depend on another? So before you measure anything — anything at all — you sort your quantities into three classes of variable.
veritasium.4: Key idea You change the independent variable, you measure the dependent variable, and you hold every controlled variable constant. That's the whole game. And because your independent variable is continuous, you don't just test it once — you measure the dependent variable at a series of values across a range, so that a relationship can actually be graphed.
veritasium.5: It's easy to use these two words as if they mean the same thing, but they don't. The methodology is the overall type of scientific investigation you've chosen; the method is the specific step-by-step procedure you actually carry out. When you've got one continuous independent variable, the right methodology is a controlled experiment: you vary the independent variable across a range while holding all controlled variables constant, and measure the dependent variable at each value. The method is where you get concrete — the equipment, the values of the independent variable to be tested, the number of repeated measurements at each value, and the instruments used.
veritasium.6: What the method produces is primary data — data you collected first-hand, not pulled from someone else's paper. That data comes in two flavours: quantitative, the numerical measurements such as a time in seconds or a field strength in millitesla, and qualitative, the observed properties that aren't numbers at all, such as the colour of a fringe or whether a surface is reflective. With a continuous independent variable, the quantitative data does the heavy lifting, and you record the qualitative observations where they back up the analysis.
veritasium.7: Before you collect anything, you apply the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines. A risk assessment names the hazards and the controls that bring them down: eye protection where projectiles or lasers are in play, safe handling of masses and electrical supplies, and limits on laser power and exposure. And here's the part that's easy to forget under exam pressure — ethical conduct means honestly recording all your results, including the ones that don't match your hypothesis, and acknowledging every source and every bit of help you had.
veritasium.8: You might be tempted to write things up neatly afterwards from memory. Don't. Every piece of primary data, every observation, every procedure and every change to the method goes into a logbook at the moment it happens. It's dated and kept in chronological order — and that's not bureaucracy, it's what authenticates the data as your own first-hand work, and it's the record you'll later process and report from.
veritasium.9: Connects The physics you're investigating comes straight out of Units 3 and 4. A fields investigation might measure field strength against distance (topic 5, topic 7); a motion investigation might measure acceleration or period; a light investigation might measure fringe spacing or intensity (topic 13). Whichever context you pick, the design conventions on this page still apply.
veritasium.10: Every measurement you make is secretly being compared against something you can never actually see: the true value — the value you'd find if you could measure the quantity perfectly. You can't know it exactly. Nobody can. But the whole language of measurement quality exists to describe how close your result is likely to be to that hidden true value, and how you got there.
veritasium.11: Accuracy is a qualitative description — nothing more. A measurement is accurate if it's close to the true value, and you call it more accurate or less accurate; you never write it as a number. Precision is a completely different thing: it's how close your repeated measurements are to each other, the spread of the readings. And here's the catch that trips people up — precision tells you nothing about closeness to the true value, so it's entirely separate from accuracy. You can have a set of readings that's precise but inaccurate: they agree beautifully with one another and are all wrong by the same amount.
veritasium.12: Trap You almost certainly want to treat accuracy and precision as synonyms. They are not, and you cannot swap them. Precision is the agreement among repeated measurements; accuracy is closeness to the true value. A tightly clustered set of readings is precise — but whether it's also accurate depends entirely on where the true value sits. State accuracy qualitatively, more or less accurate, and never as a number.
veritasium.13: The resolution of an instrument is the smallest change in the measured quantity that actually shows up as a change in the reading. For a millimetre ruler that's 1 mm; for a stopwatch reading to 0.01 s, it's 0.01 s. And here's where people slip: resolution is a property of the scale on the instrument — it is not the same thing as the uncertainty of a measurement you make with it.
veritasium.14: These two words look interchangeable and aren't. A measurement error is the difference between your result and the true value. Uncertainty is a different idea entirely: it's the interval within which the true value is expected to lie. So when you write a result as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty — say 20 ± 1 mm — you're saying the true value is expected to lie between 19 mm and 21 mm. On a graph, that interval becomes an uncertainty bar.
veritasium.15: Trap Resolution is not uncertainty — even though they're easy to conflate. The resolution of a millimetre ruler is 1 mm, but by VCE convention its measurement uncertainty is half the smallest scale increment, ±0.5 mm. Quote the full increment, or blur the two terms together, and you've made one of the most common errors there is.
veritasium.16: There are two kinds of error hiding in your measurements, and they do their damage in completely different ways.
veritasium.17: Trap You might assume averaging cleans up every kind of error. It doesn't — it only reduces random error. Systematic error shifts all your readings the same way, so taking more readings and averaging them does absolutely nothing to it. The only way to kill a systematic error is to find its cause and correct it — zero the instrument, eliminate the parallax.
veritasium.18: Repeatability is the closeness of agreement between successive measurements made under the same conditions — same observer, instrument, method and location, over a short time. It's evaluating precision. Reproducibility asks the harder question: how closely do the measurements agree when you deliberately change the conditions — a different observer, instrument, method or location? Because it stress-tests your claimed precision and can flush out systematic error, reproducibility links to accuracy.
veritasium.19: An investigation is valid if it actually investigates what it set out to investigate — which sounds obvious until you see how easily it breaks. Validity is threatened the moment a controlled variable is allowed to drift, because then you can no longer pin the change in the dependent variable on the independent variable. It's also threatened by observer bias, where the observer's own expectations quietly bend the readings.
veritasium.20: Key idea Random error scatters your readings and harms precision — and you beat it back by repeating and averaging. Systematic error shifts every reading the same way and harms accuracy — and averaging does nothing for it. Repeatability and reproducibility are about precision; validity is the separate question of whether your experiment tests what it claims to test.
veritasium.21: You don't get to write down as many digits as your calculator shows. Calculated results are stated to a number of significant figures that's consistent with the precision of the data — and two rules decide that for you.
veritasium.22: And here's the discipline most people get wrong: you round only at the very final step. Keep your intermediate results carrying extra figures, so the rounding doesn't quietly pile up on itself along the way.
veritasium.23: Trap The two significant-figure rules are not interchangeable, even though it's tempting to apply one everywhere. Addition and subtraction are governed by decimal places; multiplication and division are governed by significant figures. And rounding at each intermediate step, instead of only at the end, introduces error you never needed to have.
veritasium.24: By VCE convention, here's the rule you reach for every time: the uncertainty of a reading taken with an analogue or digital instrument is half its smallest scale increment. A millimetre ruler has a smallest increment of 1 mm, so the uncertainty is ±0.5 mm. A stopwatch reading to 0.01 s has an uncertainty of ±0.005 s. And you report the result as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty:
veritasium.25: The percentage uncertainty reframes the uncertainty as a fraction of the measurement itself — which is what lets you fairly compare the relative quality of two measurements that are nothing alike in size:
veritasium.26: Say you measure a length with a millimetre ruler and read 152 mm. The smallest scale increment is 1 mm, so the uncertainty is half of that — ±0.5 mm.
veritasium.27: Result: the length is 152.0 ± 0.5 mm. What that's really telling you is that the true value is expected to lie between 151.5 mm and 152.5 mm.
veritasium.28: Percentage uncertainty: 0.5152.0 × 100 = 0.33%, stated to two significant figures.
veritasium.29: Now suppose a trolley travels 1.27 m in 0.42 s, and you need the average speed. This is a division, so the answer keeps the fewest significant figures of the values you fed in. The 1.27 m has three significant figures and the 0.42 s has two — so two significant figures is where your answer lands.
veritasium.30: Speed = 1.270.42 = 3.0238… = 3.0 m s⁻¹. And notice — the rounding happens only at this final step.
veritasium.31: Key idea State every result as a mean ± uncertainty, with the uncertainty equal to half the smallest scale increment. Apply decimal places when you add or subtract, significant figures when you multiply or divide, and round only at the final step.
veritasium.32: A graph is where the relationship between your two variables finally shows itself. The independent variable goes on the horizontal axis, the dependent variable on the vertical axis. Each axis gets its quantity and unit, and you scale it so the data fill the available area rather than huddling in one corner. Every plotted point carries uncertainty bars — a vertical bar for the uncertainty in the dependent variable, a horizontal bar for the uncertainty in the independent variable. The true value for each point is expected to lie somewhere inside its bars.
veritasium.33: When the relationship is linear, you draw a line of best fit — not connecting the dots, but passing as close as possible to all of them, with points scattered evenly above and below. It should pass within the uncertainty bars of the points. And there's a list of tempting mistakes to avoid: don't draw the line through only the first and last points, don't force it through some chosen point or through the origin, and don't extend it beyond the range of your data.
veritasium.34: Trap A line of best fit is not drawn through the first and last points only, is not forced through the origin, and is not extrapolated beyond the data. And here's a subtle payoff: if a line you'd expect on theoretical grounds to pass through the origin doesn't, that displacement isn't noise — it's evidence of a systematic error.
veritasium.35: What do you do when the raw variables don't fall on a straight line at all? You linearise: you transform one variable so that a plot of the transformed data is a straight line. And both the variable and its axis unit have to be transformed — you can't change one and leave the other. If a quantity is expected to depend on the square of the time, you square the values of time and label the axis with the squared quantity and its unit, for example “time² (s²)”. The payoff is that the gradient of the linearised graph now equals a physical quantity.
veritasium.36: The gradient of a straight-line graph is the rise divided by the run — but read it from two points that lie on the line and are chosen far apart, not from points lifted straight out of your data table:
veritasium.37: The units of the gradient are the units of the vertical axis divided by the units of the horizontal axis. And watch for the trap on the axes: any ×10ⁿ scale factor printed on an axis has to be included when you read off values. One more thing — you do not nudge the gradient to match a known constant.
veritasium.38: Trap Read the gradient from two widely spaced points that lie on the line of best fit — never from points in the data table that may not sit on the line at all. Include any ×10ⁿ axis scale factors, give the gradient its correct units (vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit), and resist the urge to adjust the gradient to force agreement with an expected value.
veritasium.39: Take the period T of a simple pendulum, related to its length L by T = 2π√(L/g). Plot T against L and you get a curve — useless for reading a gradient. But square it: T² = 4π²gL, which is now linear in L. So you square the values of T and plot them on the vertical axis, labelled “period² (s²)”, against L in metres on the horizontal axis.
veritasium.40: Pick two points on the line of best fit, far apart, reading (0.20 m, 0.80 s²) and (1.00 m, 4.0 s²). The gradient is 4.0 − 0.801.00 − 0.20 = 3.20.80 = 4.0 s² m⁻¹, with units of vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit.
veritasium.41: Now here's the satisfying part. That gradient equals 4π²g, so g = 4π²gradient = 39.54.0 = 9.9 m s⁻², stated to two significant figures. The slope of a line on a page has just handed you a physical quantity.
