Read the gradient as rise over run from two widely spaced points on the line of best fit, give it the correct units, and include any axis scale factors.
veritasium.43: Your primary data is now evidence — evidence that bears on the hypothesis. You organise it, analyse it and evaluate it to find the patterns: the gradient of the linearised data, the trend of the line of best fit, the spread of the points relative to their uncertainty bars — all of it gets read as evidence. And only then does a conclusion state how strongly that evidence supports the hypothesis — after you've reckoned with the causes of uncertainty, the assumptions you made, and the limitations of your data and method.
veritasium.44: Here's the idea students resist hardest. Evidence can support or refute a hypothesis, a model or a theory — but it can never establish one with certainty. So your conclusion states that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis. The words “proves” and “disproves” don't belong here — because a finite pile of measurements can't prove a general claim, no matter how clean the data looks.
veritasium.45: Trap A conclusion never says the evidence “proves” or “disproves” the hypothesis — however much you'd like it to. The correct expressions are that the evidence supports, partially supports, or does not support the hypothesis. And “partially supports” has a precise meaning: the evidence matches the predicted trend over part of the range, or matches the trend but not the predicted value.
veritasium.46: The evaluation is where you turn a critical eye on your own work. You identify the limitations of the investigation, the assumptions it leaned on, and the causes of uncertainty in the data — then you propose improvements that would actually address them. A limitation is a specific weakness, such as a restricted range of the independent variable or a controlled variable you couldn't hold perfectly constant; an improvement is a concrete change that would reduce its effect.
veritasium.47: Finally, you report it all using the conventions of science communication: correct terminology, symbols, equations and standard abbreviations; consistent units; and results stated to an appropriate number of significant figures. Quantities are written with their units in upright form, such as m s⁻¹, and uncertainties with the ± symbol.
veritasium.48: And then the whole thing has to fit on a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. At its centre sits a single Communication statement — one sentence that answers the investigation question — and it's not a footnote: it occupies 20–25% of the poster area. Around it go the prescribed sections.
veritasium.49: On a poster, every word has to earn its place. The word limit forces you to include only what's essential, and to acknowledge all references and sources of assistance. The job is to explain your key findings and their implications clearly enough that a reader can judge for themselves how the evidence answers the question.
veritasium.50: Key idea The poster answers the investigation question in a single central Communication statement, backed by results that reveal the trend and a discussion that evaluates the evidence. The conclusion states that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis — all inside a limit of 600 words and the prescribed sections.

attenborough.0: Here a student sets out alone, designing a practical investigation that gathers its own primary data on fields, on motion, on light — varying a single continuous independent variable and watching what the dependent variable answers. In the end, it is judged as a scientific poster. In this chapter we shall watch how such an investigation is designed, how the quality of its measurements is weighed, how the data are processed and graphed, and how, at last, a defensible conclusion is communicated in the conventions the discipline demands.
attenborough.1: Every investigation begins, quietly, with a single clearly stated investigation question: a specific, answerable question about the relationship between two measurable quantities. That question becomes the very title of the poster. From it flow the rest — the aim, a statement of what the investigation sets out to determine, and the relevant physics concepts and key definitions, drawn from the study design so that the whole design rests upon established theory.
attenborough.2: A hypothesis is a testable, falsifiable prediction of the relationship between the variables — spoken before any data are gathered, and justified by the background physics. It must commit to a direction, a form: that the period of a pendulum increases with its length, perhaps, or that the magnetic field strength decreases with distance from a magnet. A prediction, in turn, states the expected outcome of the specific procedure.
attenborough.3: An investigation in this area of study asks how one quantity depends upon another — and so, before a single measurement is taken, three classes of variable are identified.
attenborough.4: Key idea The investigator changes the independent variable, measures the dependent variable, and holds every controlled variable constant. With one continuous independent variable, the dependent variable is measured at a series of values across a range, so that a relationship may at last be graphed.
attenborough.5: The methodology is the overall type of scientific investigation chosen; the method is the specific step-by-step procedure carried out. For an investigation with one continuous independent variable, the fitting methodology is a controlled experiment: the independent variable is varied across a range while all controlled variables are held constant, and the dependent variable is measured at each value. The method, for its part, sets out the equipment, the values of the independent variable to be tested, the number of repeated measurements at each value, and the instruments used.
attenborough.6: From the method comes primary data — data gathered first-hand by the investigator. Such data may be quantitative (numerical measurements, such as a time in seconds or a field strength in millitesla) or qualitative (observed properties that carry no number, such as the colour of a fringe or whether a surface is reflective). An investigation with a continuous independent variable leans upon quantitative primary data, with qualitative observations recorded wherever they support the analysis.
attenborough.7: Before any data are collected, the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines are applied. A risk assessment names the hazards and the controls that tame them: eye protection where projectiles or lasers are used, safe handling of masses and electrical supplies, and limits on laser power and exposure. And ethical conduct asks something quieter still — the honest recording of all results, even those that do not match the hypothesis, and the acknowledgement of every source and every helping hand.
attenborough.8: All primary data, all observations, every procedure and each change to the method are set down in a logbook at the very moment they occur. The logbook is dated and kept in chronological order; it authenticates the primary data as the investigator’s own first-hand work, and it provides the record from which results are later processed and reported.
attenborough.9: Connects The physics under investigation is drawn from Units 3 and 4: a fields investigation may measure field strength against distance (topic 5, topic 7), a motion investigation may measure acceleration or period, and a light investigation may measure fringe spacing or intensity (topic 13). Whichever context is chosen, the design conventions on this page hold true.
attenborough.10: Every measurement is weighed against the true value: the value that would be found if the quantity could ever be measured perfectly. The true value can never be known exactly — yet the language of measurement quality describes how near a result is likely to lie to it, and how that result came to be.
attenborough.11: Accuracy is a qualitative description: a measurement is accurate if it lies close to the true value. Accuracy is spoken of as more accurate or less accurate; it is never given as a number. Precision is something else — how closely repeated measurements cluster about one another, the spread of the readings. Precision says nothing of closeness to the true value, and so it stands apart from accuracy. A set of readings may be precise yet inaccurate, agreeing closely among themselves and yet all wandering far from the true value.
attenborough.12: Trap Beware: accuracy and precision are not synonyms, and must never be traded for one another. Precision is the agreement among repeated measurements; accuracy is closeness to the true value. A clustered set of readings is precise; whether it is also accurate depends entirely on where the true value lies. State accuracy qualitatively — more or less accurate — and never as a number.
attenborough.13: The resolution of an instrument is the smallest change in the measured quantity that stirs a perceptible change in the reading. The resolution of a millimetre ruler is 1 mm; the resolution of a stopwatch reading to 0.01 s is 0.01 s. Resolution is a property of the scale alone — and it is not the same as the uncertainty of a measurement made with the instrument.
attenborough.14: A measurement error is the difference between a measurement result and the true value. Uncertainty is a different creature: it is the interval within which the true value is expected to lie. A result written as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty, such as 20 ± 1 mm, declares that the true value is expected to lie between 19 mm and 21 mm. On a graph this interval is drawn as an uncertainty bar.
attenborough.15: Trap Beware: resolution is not uncertainty. The resolution of a millimetre ruler is 1 mm, yet by VCE convention its measurement uncertainty is half the smallest scale increment, ±0.5 mm. To state the uncertainty as the full increment, or to confuse the two terms, is a common and avoidable error.
attenborough.16: Two kinds of error trouble measurements, and each in its own way.
attenborough.17: Trap Beware: averaging reduces random error only. Systematic error touches every reading equally, and so taking more readings and averaging them will never drive it out. A systematic error is banished only by finding and correcting its cause — by zeroing the instrument, perhaps, or by eliminating parallax.
attenborough.18: Repeatability is the closeness of agreement between successive measurements made under the same conditions: the same observer, the same instrument, method and location, over a short span of time. Repeatability evaluates precision. Reproducibility is the closeness of agreement when the conditions are changed: a different observer, instrument, method or location. Reproducibility tests claimed precision and can uncover systematic error, and so it reaches back to accuracy.
attenborough.19: An investigation is valid if it investigates what it sets out to investigate. Validity is imperilled if controlled variables are let to drift, so that the change in the dependent variable can no longer be laid at the door of the independent variable — or by observer bias, in which the observer’s own expectations steal into the readings.
attenborough.20: Key idea Random error scatters the readings and wounds precision; it is tamed by repeating and averaging. Systematic error shifts every reading the same way and wounds accuracy; averaging cannot mend it. Repeatability and reproducibility evaluate precision; validity is whether the experiment tests what it claims to test.
attenborough.21: Calculated results are stated to a number of significant figures in keeping with the precision of the data. Two rules govern the calculations.
attenborough.22: Rounding is left to the very last step. The intermediate results are kept to extra figures, so that rounding may never accumulate.
attenborough.23: Trap Beware: the two significant-figure rules are not interchangeable. Addition and subtraction are governed by decimal places; multiplication and division by significant figures. To round at each intermediate step, rather than only at the end, introduces an avoidable error.
attenborough.24: By VCE convention, the uncertainty of a reading taken with an analogue or digital instrument is half its smallest scale increment. A millimetre ruler has a smallest increment of 1 mm, giving an uncertainty of ±0.5 mm. A stopwatch reading to 0.01 s carries an uncertainty of ±0.005 s. And a result is reported, always, as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty:
attenborough.25: The percentage uncertainty expresses the uncertainty as a fraction of the measurement, and so allows the relative quality of different measurements to be set side by side:
attenborough.26: A length is measured with a millimetre ruler as 152 mm. The smallest scale increment is 1 mm, so the uncertainty is half of this, ±0.5 mm.
attenborough.27: Result: the length is 152.0 ± 0.5 mm. The true value is expected to lie between 151.5 mm and 152.5 mm.
attenborough.28: Percentage uncertainty: 0.5152.0 × 100 = 0.33%, stated to two significant figures.
attenborough.29: A trolley travels 1.27 m in 0.42 s, and we seek the average speed. The calculation is a division, so the answer keeps the fewest significant figures of the values used. The value 1.27 m carries three significant figures and 0.42 s carries two, so the answer is stated to two significant figures.
attenborough.30: Speed = 1.270.42 = 3.0238… = 3.0 m s⁻¹. Rounding is applied only at this final step.
attenborough.31: Key idea State every result as a mean ± uncertainty, with the uncertainty equal to half the smallest scale increment. Apply decimal places when adding or subtracting, and significant figures when multiplying or dividing, and round only at the final step.
attenborough.32: A graph lays bare the relationship between the independent and dependent variables. The independent variable is plotted on the horizontal axis, the dependent variable on the vertical. Each axis is labelled with its quantity and unit, and scaled so that the data fill the space available. Each plotted point bears uncertainty bars: a vertical bar shows the uncertainty in the dependent variable, a horizontal bar the uncertainty in the independent variable. The true value for each point is expected to lie within its bars.
attenborough.33: When the relationship is linear, a line of best fit is drawn so that it passes as near as possible to all the points, with the points scattered evenly above and below it. The line of best fit should pass within the uncertainty bars of the points. And there are errors to be avoided: the line must not be drawn through only the first and last points, must not be forced through a chosen point or through the origin, and must not be extended beyond the range of the data.
attenborough.34: Trap Beware: a line of best fit is not drawn through the first and last points only, is not forced through the origin, and is not extrapolated beyond the data. And if a line that is expected on theoretical grounds to pass through the origin does not, that displacement is itself evidence of a systematic error.
attenborough.35: When the relationship between the raw variables is no straight line, the data are linearised: one variable is transformed, so that a plot of the transformed data falls into a straight line. Both the variable and its axis unit must be transformed together. If a quantity is expected to depend on the square of the time, the values of time are squared and the axis is labelled with the squared quantity and its unit, for example “time² (s²)”. The gradient of the linearised graph then equals a physical quantity.
attenborough.36: The gradient of a straight-line graph is the rise divided by the run, read from two points that lie on the line and are chosen far apart — never from points taken straight from the data table:
attenborough.37: The units of the gradient are the units of the vertical axis divided by the units of the horizontal axis. Any ×10ⁿ scale factor printed on an axis must be carried in when reading values. And the gradient is never adjusted to match a known constant.
attenborough.38: Trap Beware: read the gradient from two widely spaced points that lie on the line of best fit, never from points in the data table that may not lie on the line. Carry in any ×10ⁿ axis scale factors, give the gradient its correct units (vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit), and do not adjust the gradient to force agreement with an expected value.
attenborough.39: The period T of a simple pendulum is bound to its length L by T = 2π√(L/g), and so a plot of T against L is a curve. Squaring gives T² = 4π²gL, which is linear in L. The values of T are squared and plotted on the vertical axis, labelled “period² (s²)”, against L in metres on the horizontal axis.
attenborough.40: Two points on the line of best fit, chosen far apart, are read as (0.20 m, 0.80 s²) and (1.00 m, 4.0 s²). The gradient is 4.0 − 0.801.00 − 0.20 = 3.20.80 = 4.0 s² m⁻¹, with units of vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit.
attenborough.41: The gradient equals 4π²g, and so g = 4π²gradient = 39.54.0 = 9.9 m s⁻², stated to two significant figures. From the gradient of the linearised graph, a physical quantity has emerged.
attenborough.42: Key idea Linearise by transforming the variable and its axis unit together, so that the gradient of the straight line equals a physical quantity. Read the gradient as rise over run from two widely spaced points on the line of best fit, give it the correct units, and carry in any axis scale factors.
attenborough.43: Primary data are the evidence that bears upon the hypothesis. The evidence is organised, analysed and evaluated for its patterns; the gradient of linearised data, the trend of the line of best fit, the spread of the points against their uncertainty bars — all of these are read as evidence. A conclusion states how strongly that evidence supports the hypothesis, once the causes of uncertainty, the assumptions made, and the limitations of the data and method have all been weighed.
attenborough.44: Evidence can support or refute a hypothesis, a model, a theory — but it can never establish one with certainty. And so a conclusion states that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis. The words “proves” and “disproves” are never spoken, for a finite set of measurements can never prove a general claim.
attenborough.45: Trap Beware: a conclusion never states that the evidence “proves” or “disproves” the hypothesis. The right expressions are that the evidence supports, partially supports, or does not support the hypothesis. Evidence that partially supports a hypothesis matches the predicted trend over part of the range, or matches the trend but not the predicted value.
attenborough.46: The evaluation names the limitations of the investigation, the assumptions on which it leaned, and the causes of uncertainty in the data — and then proposes the improvements that would address them. A limitation is a specific weakness, such as a restricted range of the independent variable, or a controlled variable that could not be held perfectly constant; an improvement is a concrete change that would lessen its effect.
attenborough.47: The investigation is reported in the conventions of science communication: correct terminology, symbols, equations and standard abbreviations; consistent units; and results stated to a fitting number of significant figures. Quantities are written with their units in upright form, such as m s⁻¹, and uncertainties with the ± symbol.
attenborough.48: The investigation is judged as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. At its very heart sits a single Communication statement: one sentence that answers the investigation question. This statement takes up 20–25% of the poster area. And around it the poster carries the prescribed sections.
attenborough.49: Communication on a poster is spare: the word limit demands that only the essential survives, and that all references and sources of assistance are acknowledged. The key findings and their implications are made plain enough that a reader may judge, for themselves, how the evidence answers the question.
attenborough.50: Key idea The poster answers the investigation question in a single central Communication statement, upheld by results that reveal the trend and a discussion that evaluates the evidence. The conclusion states that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis — within a limit of 600 words and the prescribed sections.

greekepic.0: Sing, Muse, of the labour the young investigator undertakes alone: a practical inquiry that brings forth primary data upon fields, upon motion, or upon light, governed by a single continuous independent variable, and judged at the last as a scientific poster. greekepic.1: Every scientific inquiry takes its first breath from a clearly proclaimed investigation question: one specific question, answerable, concerning the bond between two quantities that may be measured. greekepic.2: A hypothesis is a prediction that may be tested and may be cast down — a foretelling of the bond between the variables, uttered before any datum is gathered, and justified by the background physics. A hypothesis names a direction or a form: thus, that the period of a pendulum grows greater with its length, or that the strength of a magnetic field wanes with distance from the magnet. A prediction declares the awaited outcome of the one specific procedure.
greekepic.3: An inquiry within this area of study tests how one quantity hangs upon another; therefore three orders of variable are named and set apart before any measurement is taken.
greekepic.4: Key idea The investigator alters the independent variable, measures the dependent variable, and holds every controlled variable unchanging. With one continuous independent variable, the dependent variable is measured at a sequence of values across a range, so that a bond may be graphed.
greekepic.5: The methodology is the whole kind of scientific inquiry that is chosen; the method is the particular step-by-step procedure that is carried out. For an inquiry bearing one continuous independent variable, the fitting methodology is a controlled experiment: the independent variable is varied across a range while all controlled variables are held unchanging, and the dependent variable is measured at each value. The method names the equipment, the values of the independent variable to be put to the test, the number of measurements repeated at each value, and the instruments employed.
greekepic.6: The method brings forth primary data, data gathered first-hand by the investigator's own hand. Primary data may be quantitative (measurements in number, such as a time in seconds or a field strength in millitesla) or qualitative (properties observed yet bearing no number, such as the colour of a fringe or whether a surface casts back the light). An inquiry with a continuous independent variable leans upon quantitative primary data, with qualitative observations set down wherever they lend strength to the analysis.
greekepic.7: Before any datum is gathered, the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines are brought to bear. A risk assessment names the hazards and the controls that lessen them: protection for the eyes where projectiles or lasers are loosed, the safe handling of masses and electrical supplies, and limits set upon laser power and exposure. Ethical conduct demands the honest recording of all results, even those that do not answer to the hypothesis, and the acknowledgement of all sources and all assistance.
greekepic.8: All primary data, all observations, all procedures, and every change wrought upon the method are set down in a logbook at the very hour they come to pass. The logbook is dated and kept in the order of time; it bears witness that the primary data are the investigator’s own first-hand work, and it furnishes the record from which results are afterward processed and reported.
greekepic.9: Connects The physics here put to inquiry is drawn from Units 3 and 4: a fields inquiry may measure field strength against distance (topic 5, topic 7), a motion inquiry may measure acceleration or period, and a light inquiry may measure fringe spacing or intensity (topic 13). The conventions of design proclaimed upon this page hold true for whichever context is chosen.
greekepic.10: Every measurement is weighed against the true value: that value which would be found could the quantity be measured in perfection. The true value can never be known exactly, yet the language of measurement quality tells how near a result is likely to stand to it, and by what manner the result was won.
greekepic.11: Accuracy is a description in quality: a measurement is accurate if it stands close to the true value. Accuracy is spoken of as more accurate or less accurate; it is never proclaimed as a number. Precision is how close repeated measurements stand to one another, that is, the spread of the readings. Precision gives no sign of closeness to the true value, and so it stands apart from accuracy. A set of readings may be precise yet inaccurate, if they agree closely each with each, and yet all lie far from the true value.
greekepic.12: Trap Mark well: accuracy and precision are not the same word, and must never be exchanged the one for the other. cnpop.0: 学生自己设计的探究实验会围绕场、运动或光产出一手数据，用一个连续变化的自变量来跑，最后做成一张科学海报来打分。这一章就是手把手教你：怎么设计这场探究、怎么判断你测的数据靠不靠谱、怎么处理数据画图，最后怎么用规定的套路把一个站得住脚的结论给写明白。
cnpop.1: 一场科学探究的起手式，是一个写得清清楚楚的investigation question（探究问题）：一个具体、能回答的问题，问的是两个可测量之间到底啥关系。这个问题会直接变成海报的标题。顺着它往下走，就有了aim（目的），也就是这场探究到底想搞清楚啥；还有相关的physics concepts and key definitions（物理概念和关键定义），这些都从study design里扒出来，好让整个设计稳稳踩在已有理论上面。
cnpop.2: hypothesis（假设）是一个能检验、能被证伪的预测，说的是变量之间的关系，必须在收数据之前就甩出来，而且得有背景物理给它撑腰。假设得说清方向或者形式：比如说，摆的周期随长度增大而增大，或者磁场强度随着离磁铁越远而减小。而prediction（预测）说的是某个具体步骤会跑出啥结果。
cnpop.3: 这个学习领域里的探究，考的就是一个量怎么随另一个量变，所以在动手测任何东西之前，先把三类变量给认出来。
cnpop.4: Key idea 探究者去改自变量，去测因变量，然后把每一个受控变量死死按住不变。当你只有一个连续的自变量时，就要在一整个范围里取一系列的值，每个值都测一下因变量，这样才能把关系画成图。
cnpop.5: methodology（方法论）是你选的那种科学探究的大类；method（方法）是你实际一步一步走的具体流程。对于只有一个连续自变量的探究，对得上号的方法论是controlled experiment（受控实验）：自变量在一个范围里变来变去，所有受控变量统统按住不动，每取一个值就测一次因变量。method则要把这些都写明白：用啥器材、自变量要测哪些值、每个值重复测几次、用的是啥仪器。
cnpop.6: method会产出primary data（一手数据），也就是探究者亲手第一手收来的数据。一手数据可以是quantitative（定量的，数值型测量，比如以秒为单位的时间、以毫特斯拉为单位的场强），也可以是qualitative（定性的，不是数字的那种观察属性，比如条纹的颜色、某个表面反不反光）。一场带连续自变量的探究主要靠的是定量一手数据，定性观察就在能帮上分析的地方顺手记下来。
cnpop.7: 收数据之前，先把相关的health, safety and ethical guidelines（健康、安全和伦理准则）安排上。风险评估要把危险源和压制它们的措施都列出来：有抛射物或激光的地方戴好护目镜，砝码和电源要安全操作，激光功率和暴露量要设限。伦理这块要求你诚实记录所有结果——包括那些跟假设对不上的，还要把所有来源和别人给的帮助都老实交代清楚。
cnpop.8: 所有一手数据、观察、流程，还有对method的改动，都要在发生的当下就记进logbook（实验记录本）里。记录本要写日期、按时间顺序排好；它能证明这些一手数据确实是探究者自己第一手干出来的活，也是之后处理和汇报结果时的依据底本。

cnpop.15: Trap 划重点：分辨率不是不确定度。毫米尺的分辨率是 1 mm，但按VCE的约定，它的测量不确定度是最小刻度的一半，也就是 ±0.5 mm。把不确定度写成整个刻度增量，或者把这俩术语搞混，是非常常见的翻车现场。
cnpop.16: 有两种误差，它们坑你的方式各不相同。

cnpop.36: 一条直线图的gradient是rise除以run，从两个落在线上、而且挑得离得很远的点来读，不是从数据表里随便抓两个点：

cnpop.44: 证据可以支持或者推翻一个假设、模型或理论，但它没法把任何一个板上钉钉地确立下来。所以结论只能说证据supports（支持）、partially supports（部分支持）或者does not support（不支持）这个假设。“proves”（证明）和“disproves”（证伪）这两个词不能用，因为有限的一堆测量没法证明一个普遍性的论断。
cnpop.45: Trap 划重点：结论永远不能说证据“proves”（证明）或者“disproves”（证伪）了假设。正确的说法是证据supports（支持）、partially supports（部分支持）或者does not support（不支持）假设。所谓部分支持一个假设，是说证据在部分范围内对上了预测的趋势，或者对上了趋势但没对上预测的值。
cnpop.46: 评估这一步要把探究的limitations（局限）、它依赖的假设、以及数据里不确定度的来源都揪出来，然后提出能对症下药的improvements（改进）。一个局限是某个具体的软肋，比如自变量的范围被限得太窄，或者某个受控变量没法被完美地按住不变；一个改进是某个能压住它影响的具体动作。
cnpop.47: 汇报这场探究要按科学交流的套路来：术语、符号、方程和标准缩写都要写对；单位要前后一致；结果要保留合适的有效数字位数。量要带着它们的单位写成正体，比如 m s⁻¹，不确定度要用 ± 符号。
cnpop.48: 这场探究最后是以一张不超过 600 字的scientific poster（科学海报）来打分的。海报的正中央是唯一一句Communication statement（传达陈述）：用一句话回答探究问题。这句陈述要占掉海报面积的 20–25%。海报上还得带着规定的那些版块。
cnpop.49: 海报上的交流讲究一个干脆利落：字数限制逼着你只放真正必要的东西，所有参考文献和别人给的帮助都要交代清楚。关键发现和它们的意义要讲得足够明白，明白到一个读者能自己判断证据是怎么回答那个问题的。
cnpop.50: Key idea 海报用正中央唯一一句Communication statement回答探究问题，背后由揭示趋势的结果和评估证据的讨论来撑着。结论要说证据supports（支持）、partially supports（部分支持）或者does not support（不支持）假设，全程控制在 600 字以内、并带齐规定的那些版块。

ai.0: Picture this: you get to ask your own question about the universe — about fields, motion or light — and then go find the answer first-hand. That’s a student-designed practical investigation. You change one continuous independent variable, watch what happens, and present the whole story as a scientific poster. This chapter walks you through it all: how to design the investigation, how to judge whether your measurements can be trusted, how to process and graph what you collect, and how to land a conclusion you can actually defend — all in the conventions the assessors expect.
ai.1: Everything starts with one sharp question. Your investigation question is a specific, answerable question about how two measurable quantities relate to each other — and it does double duty as the title of your poster. From that question flows the aim, a plain statement of what you set out to determine, together with the relevant physics concepts and key definitions you draw from the study design. Pin those down early, and your whole design stands on solid, established theory rather than guesswork.
ai.2: A hypothesis is your testable, falsifiable prediction of how the variables relate — written before you collect a single data point, and backed by the background physics. The trick is that it has to commit to a direction or a form: that the period of a pendulum increases with its length, say, or that the magnetic field strength decreases with distance from a magnet. A prediction, by contrast, says what your specific procedure should turn up.
ai.3: In this area of study you’re always testing how one quantity leans on another — so before you measure anything, you sort your quantities into three kinds of variable.
ai.4: Key idea You change the independent variable, you measure the dependent variable, and you hold every controlled variable rock-steady. Because your independent variable is continuous, you measure the dependent variable at a whole series of values across a range — that’s what lets you graph the relationship in the end.
ai.5: Two words that sound similar but aren’t. The methodology is the overall type of scientific investigation you choose; the method is the actual step-by-step procedure you carry out. With one continuous independent variable, the methodology that fits is a controlled experiment: you vary the independent variable across a range, hold all the controlled variables constant, and measure the dependent variable at each value. The method then spells out the nitty-gritty — the equipment, exactly which values of the independent variable you’ll test, how many repeats you’ll take at each one, and the instruments you’ll use.
ai.6: What your method gives you is primary data — data you collect first-hand, with your own hands and eyes. It comes in two flavours. Quantitative data is numerical: a time in seconds, a field strength in millitesla. Qualitative data is the observed stuff that isn’t a number — the colour of a fringe, or whether a surface is reflective. An investigation built on a continuous independent variable leans on quantitative primary data, but you record any qualitative observations that help you make sense of it.
ai.7: Before you collect anything, you bring in the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines. A risk assessment names the hazards and the controls that tame them: eye protection wherever projectiles or lasers are in play, safe handling of masses and electrical supplies, and limits on laser power and exposure. Ethics asks something of you too — honestly recording all your results, including the awkward ones that don’t match your hypothesis, and crediting every source and every helping hand.
ai.8: Every piece of primary data, every observation, every step of your procedure and every change you make to the method goes into a logbook — written down as it happens, not reconstructed later. You date it and keep it in chronological order. That logbook is what authenticates your primary data as your own first-hand work, and it’s the record you’ll mine when the time comes to process and report your results.
ai.9: Connects The physics you’re probing comes straight out of Units 3 and 4. A fields investigation might pit field strength against distance (topic 5, topic 7); a motion investigation might chase acceleration or period; a light investigation might measure fringe spacing or intensity (topic 13). Whichever context you pick, the design conventions on this page apply just the same.
ai.10: Every measurement you make is silently held up against the true value — the value you’d get if you could measure the quantity perfectly. You can never know that true value exactly, but the language of measurement quality gives you a way to talk about how close your result probably gets to it, and about how you arrived at the result in the first place.
ai.11: Accuracy is a qualitative call: a measurement is accurate if it sits close to the true value. You describe it as more accurate or less accurate — never as a number. Precision is a different beast: it’s how tightly your repeated measurements cluster together, the spread of your readings. Precision says nothing about closeness to the true value, which is exactly why it’s kept separate from accuracy. A set of readings can be wonderfully precise yet inaccurate — all agreeing snugly with one another while sitting, together, a long way from the truth.
ai.12: Trap Accuracy and precision are not synonyms, so don’t swap one for the other. Precision is the agreement among repeated measurements; accuracy is closeness to the true value. A tightly clustered set of readings is precise — but whether it’s also accurate depends entirely on where the true value happens to lie. State accuracy qualitatively (more or less accurate), and never as a number.
ai.13: The resolution of an instrument is the smallest change in the measured quantity that nudges the reading by something you can actually see. A millimetre ruler resolves to 1 mm; a stopwatch reading to 0.01 s resolves to 0.01 s. Resolution is a feature of the scale itself — and it is not the same thing as the uncertainty of a measurement you make with the instrument.
ai.14: A measurement error is the gap between your result and the true value. Uncertainty is a different idea entirely: it’s the interval within which you expect the true value to lie. Write a result as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty — something like 20 ± 1 mm — and you’re saying the true value is expected to fall between 19 mm and 21 mm. On a graph, you draw that interval as an uncertainty bar.
ai.15: Trap Resolution is not uncertainty. A millimetre ruler resolves to 1 mm, but by VCE convention its measurement uncertainty is half the smallest scale increment, ±0.5 mm. Quoting the uncertainty as the full increment, or muddling the two terms together, is a mistake people make all the time.
ai.16: There are two kinds of error out there, and they mess with your measurements in completely different ways.
ai.17: Trap Averaging reduces random error only. A systematic error pushes every reading the same way, so piling up more readings and averaging them won’t shift it one bit. You only kill a systematic error by hunting down its cause and fixing it — zeroing the instrument, say, or getting rid of parallax.
ai.18: Repeatability is how closely successive measurements agree when nothing changes: same observer, same instrument, same method, same location, all over a short stretch of time. Repeatability is a verdict on precision. Reproducibility is how closely measurements agree when you deliberately change the conditions: a different observer, instrument, method or location. Reproducibility puts your claimed precision to the test and can flush out systematic error — which is why it ties back to accuracy.
ai.19: An investigation is valid if it actually investigates what it claims to investigate. Validity comes under threat when controlled variables are allowed to drift — because then you can no longer pin the change in the dependent variable on the independent variable — or when observer bias creeps in and the observer’s expectations start colouring the readings.
ai.20: Key idea Random error scatters your readings and wounds precision; you tame it by repeating and averaging. Systematic error shoves every reading the same way and wounds accuracy; averaging does nothing for it. Repeatability and reproducibility are verdicts on precision; validity is the question of whether your experiment tests what it claims to test.
ai.21: You quote calculated results to a number of significant figures that matches the precision of your data. Two rules run the show.
ai.22: You round only at the very last step. Carry intermediate results to a few extra figures, so that rounding errors don’t quietly snowball on you.
ai.23: Trap The two significant-figure rules are not interchangeable. Addition and subtraction are governed by decimal places; multiplication and division are governed by significant figures. And rounding at every intermediate step, instead of only at the end, bakes in error you could easily have avoided.
ai.24: By VCE convention, the uncertainty of a reading taken with an analogue or digital instrument is half its smallest scale increment. A millimetre ruler has a smallest increment of 1 mm, so its uncertainty is ±0.5 mm. A stopwatch reading to 0.01 s carries an uncertainty of ±0.005 s. You report a result as a mean plus or minus an uncertainty:
ai.25: The percentage uncertainty expresses the uncertainty as a fraction of the measurement — handy, because it lets you compare the relative quality of measurements that are otherwise nothing alike:
ai.26: Say you measure a length with a millimetre ruler and get 152 mm. The smallest scale increment is 1 mm, so the uncertainty is half of that, ±0.5 mm.
ai.27: Result: the length is 152.0 ± 0.5 mm. You expect the true value to lie somewhere between 151.5 mm and 152.5 mm.
ai.28: Percentage uncertainty: 0.5152.0 × 100 = 0.33%, stated to two significant figures.
ai.29: A trolley travels 1.27 m in 0.42 s, and you want the average speed. The calculation is a division, so the answer keeps the fewest significant figures of the values you feed in. The value 1.27 m has three significant figures and 0.42 s has two — so you state the answer to two significant figures.
ai.30: Speed = 1.270.42 = 3.0238… = 3.0 m s⁻¹. You round only at this final step.
ai.31: Key idea State every result as a mean ± uncertainty, with the uncertainty equal to half the smallest scale increment. Use decimal places when you add or subtract and significant figures when you multiply or divide — and round only at the final step.
ai.32: A graph is where the relationship between your independent and dependent variables finally shows its face. The independent variable goes on the horizontal axis, the dependent variable on the vertical axis. Label each axis with its quantity and unit, and scale it so the data fill the available space rather than huddling in one corner. Every plotted point carries uncertainty bars: a vertical bar for the uncertainty in the dependent variable, a horizontal bar for the uncertainty in the independent variable. The true value for each point is expected to live somewhere inside its bars.
ai.33: When the relationship turns out linear, you draw a line of best fit — threading it as close as possible to all the points, with roughly as many points scattered above the line as below. It should pass within the uncertainty bars of the points. A few temptations to resist: don’t draw the line through only the first and last points, don’t force it through some chosen point or through the origin, and don’t stretch it beyond the range of your data.
ai.34: Trap A line of best fit is not drawn through the first and last points only, is not forced through the origin, and is not extrapolated beyond the data. And here’s a useful tell: if a line that theory says should pass through the origin doesn’t, that displacement is itself evidence of a systematic error.
ai.35: When the raw variables don’t plot as a straight line, you linearise: you transform one variable so that a plot of the transformed data does come out straight. Crucially, you transform both the variable and its axis unit. If a quantity is expected to depend on the square of the time, you square the values of time and label the axis with the squared quantity and its unit — for example “time² (s²)”. The gradient of that linearised graph then turns out to equal a physical quantity.
ai.36: The gradient of a straight-line graph is the rise divided by the run, read from two points that lie on the line and sit far apart — not from points yanked out of the data table:
ai.37: The units of the gradient are the units of the vertical axis divided by the units of the horizontal axis. Any ×10ⁿ scale factor printed on an axis must be folded in when you read off values. And you don’t tweak the gradient to make it match a known constant — let it say what it says.
ai.38: Trap Read the gradient from two widely spaced points that lie on the line of best fit, never from points in the data table that might not sit on the line at all. Fold in any ×10ⁿ axis scale factors, give the gradient its correct units (vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit), and don’t massage the gradient to force agreement with the value you were hoping for.
ai.39: The period T of a simple pendulum ties to its length L through T = 2π√(L/g), so a plot of T against L comes out as a curve. Square it, though, and you get T² = 4π²gL, which is beautifully linear in L. So you square the values of T, plot them up the vertical axis labelled “period² (s²)”, against L in metres along the horizontal axis.
ai.40: Two points on the line of best fit, chosen far apart, read as (0.20 m, 0.80 s²) and (1.00 m, 4.0 s²). The gradient is 4.0 − 0.801.00 − 0.20 = 3.20.80 = 4.0 s² m⁻¹, with units of vertical-axis unit divided by horizontal-axis unit.
ai.41: That gradient equals 4π²g, so g = 4π²gradient = 39.54.0 = 9.9 m s⁻², stated to two significant figures. The gradient of a linearised graph has just handed you a physical quantity — the acceleration due to gravity, no less.
ai.42: Key idea Linearise by transforming the variable and its axis unit together, so that the gradient of the straight line equals a physical quantity. Read that gradient as rise over run from two widely spaced points on the line of best fit, give it the correct units, and fold in any axis scale factors.
ai.43: Your primary data is evidence — evidence that bears directly on your hypothesis. You organise it, analyse it and evaluate it to dig out the patterns: the gradient of your linearised data, the trend of your line of best fit, and how the points scatter relative to their uncertainty bars are all read as evidence. Then your conclusion says how strongly that evidence supports the hypothesis — but only after you’ve weighed up the causes of uncertainty, the assumptions you made, and the limitations of your data and method.
ai.44: Evidence can support a hypothesis, model or theory — or it can refute one — but it can never establish one with certainty. So your conclusion says that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis. The words “proves” and “disproves” stay out of it, because a finite handful of measurements simply cannot prove a general claim.
ai.45: Trap A conclusion never says the evidence “proves” or “disproves” the hypothesis. The right phrasings are that the evidence supports, partially supports, or does not support it. Evidence that partially supports a hypothesis matches the predicted trend over part of the range, or follows the trend but misses the predicted value.
ai.46: The evaluation is where you own up — naming the limitations of your investigation, the assumptions you leaned on, and the causes of uncertainty in your data, and then proposing improvements that would deal with them. A limitation is a specific weakness — a too-narrow range of the independent variable, say, or a controlled variable you couldn’t quite hold perfectly constant. An improvement is a concrete change that would shrink its effect.
ai.47: You report the investigation in the conventions of science communication: correct terminology, symbols, equations and standard abbreviations; consistent units; and results quoted to an appropriate number of significant figures. Quantities are written with their units in upright form — such as m s⁻¹ — and uncertainties carry the ± symbol.
ai.48: The whole thing is assessed as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. Right at its heart sits a single Communication statement: one sentence that answers the investigation question. That statement claims 20–25% of the poster area — it’s the star of the show. Around it, the poster carries the prescribed sections.
ai.49: On a poster, communication has to be lean: the word limit means only the essential material earns a place, and every reference and source of assistance gets acknowledged. You explain your key findings and what they imply clearly enough that a reader can judge, for themselves, how the evidence answers your question.
ai.50: Key idea The poster answers the investigation question in a single central Communication statement, backed by results that reveal the trend and a discussion that evaluates the evidence. The conclusion states that the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis — all within a limit of 600 words and the prescribed sections.
